name: Attack Shark R85HE
text:
The Attack Shark R85HE is a 75% keyboard aimed directly at the gamer who needs low latency along with the features that HE(Hall Effect) keys provide.

The R85HE is packaged with a keycap puller, stickers, a strap(for some reason), and USB Type-C cable.  It has a 75% form factor construction made slightly larger by the light bar and plastic shroud at the top of the keyboard.  This can display RGB patterns which are somewhat diminished with our green version but will likely look good for other models.

We found it to be quite light which can be useful for portability, but it doesn't have the weight and grip to stay in one spot if pushed around.  For being a light ABS chassis, there is very little deck flex.  The volume knob follows this trend being sturdy and having no wobble; though it cannot be reprogrammed to another function.  The slightly textured PBT keycaps feel good for typing and the double-shot legend will stand the test of time.

A downside of this keyboard is that it must be connected to the device through the USB Type-C port on the left side and there are no wireless connectivity options.  This is a high polling rate(8 kHz) and low latency(< 2 ms) connection, but limits the use cases.

The included software provides control through QMK compatibility and allows you to adjust backlighting, macros, and HE specific features.  We found it to be usable but not always the most intuitive.  It'll certainly be sufficient for occasional use.  The backlighting also features quite a few dynamic and reactive patterns without being too bright or distracting.

Our key stroke test apparatus is currently down so we have not been able to test the force profile, but the included Outemu magnetic switches felt very typical for an HE switch and have a fairly light actuation force.

The lack of wireless modes shouldn't be an issue to gamers as we measured the keyboard to have a latency of 1.46 ms.  The single outlier result in our testing was a latency value of 1.64 ms.  This will vary slightly as HE actuation distances and other settings are adjusted, but is in the top tier of keyboard latency.

name: Yunzii C75 Cake Meow
text:
The Yunzii C75 Cake Meow is a 75% tri-mode keyboard that focuses on form while also providing functionality for a currently listed cost of around $100 USD.

One of the main draws of this keyboard is the unique construction and the included accessories.  The box includes the expected complement of connection accessories, spare keys and keycaps, and a cupcake mousepad.  The cable is braided and the mousepad doesn't have a stitched-edge, but is otherwise high quality.

The entire plastic chassis has a silicone shell which feels very high quality and the PBT MOA keycaps are easy to type on.  The keycaps feature unique graphics, but are not shine-through, which would have been nice to pair with the RGB lighting surround.

The C75 Cake Meow features tri-mode connectivity as well as Bluetooth multi-pairing.  This should suit every connection need, and there is storage for the fish-themed 2.4 GHz wireless dongle.

While this keyboard doesn't support QMK or VIA, it provides it's own web interface for profile, macro, and RGB customization.  The software interface is adequate, including neat features like lighting themes(including audio reactivity), and makes it simple enough to set up profiles or program macros.

This keyboard is compatible with macOS and the included extra keycaps will support the different modifier keys.

Our key stroke test apparatus is currently down so we have not been able to test the force profile, but our unit has the YUNZII Milk V2 Linear switches and they felt smooth and relatively light with a 40g actuation force.

The gasket mounted PCB and plastic/silicone frame provide a subjectively nice sound for quick typing or general use.

The wired mode had the lowest average latency in our testing with an average of just under 6 ms.  This isn't the peak of competitive results, but is sufficient for productivity and most gaming use.  The latency through the 2.4 GHz wireless dongle is reasonable at just under 10 ms, and as expected, Bluetooth latency is higher, around 20 ms.

name: Sapphire PULSE Radeon RX 9060 XT OC 16GB
text:
The RX 9060 XT 16GB offers reasonable VRAM and a good value if it can be found for less than the RTX 5060 Ti.

The RX 9060 XT's processor is a variant of the Navi 44 processor manufactured with TSMC's N4P process node. This graphics card comes with a PCIe 5.0 x16 interface and 256-bit memory bus with its GDDR6 memory rated for 640 GB/s memory bandwidth. 

The RX 9060 XT has 2048 streaming multiprocessors, 32 ray tracing units, and 64 AI accelerators. Sapphire's OC model boosts to a whopping 3290 MHz within a 170W power envelope. We did see transient spikes as high as 225 W, but most modern power supplies(ATX 3) will handle these spikes without issue.

AMD FidelityFX Super Resolution 4 (FSR4) is now available, but only on 9000 series (RDNA4) cards. It provides a notable quality uplift in the games that support it.

Video encoding has improved over last generation, and it encodes in true 1920x1080,

not 1920x1082

. This generation does not support 10-bit 4:2:2 encoding so that may make this card less desirable for video professionals, but it does have an improved quality of encoding at lower bitrates.

At 1080p the RX 9060 XT is just behind the RTX 5060 Ti, close enough to call it comparable, this trend continues at 1440p. Ray tracing enthusiasts will be happy with AMD's progress on this front, but NVIDIA still wins in this arena.

name: Sapphire PULSE Radeon RX 9060 XT OC 8GB
text:
The RX 9060 XT 8GB has half of the VRAM of the $50 more expensive next step up and isn't a great bargain.

The RX 9060 XT's processor is a variant of the Navi 44 processor manufactured with TSMC's N4P process node. This graphics card comes with a PCIe 5.0 x16 interface and 256-bit memory bus with its GDDR6 memory rated for 640 GB/s memory bandwidth. 

The RX 9060 XT has 2048 streaming multiprocessors, 32 ray tracing units, and 64 AI accelerators. Sapphire's OC model boosts to a whopping 3290 MHz within a 170W power envelope. We did see transient spikes as high as 225 W, but most modern power supplies(ATX 3) will handle these spikes without issue.

AMD FidelityFX Super Resolution 4 (FSR4) is now available, but only on 9000 series (RDNA4) cards. It provides a notable quality uplift in the games that support it.

Video encoding has improved over last generation, and it encodes in true 1920x1080,

not 1920x1082

. This generation does not support 10-bit 4:2:2 encoding so that may make this card less desirable for video professionals, but it does have an improved quality of encoding at lower bitrates.

At 1080p the RX 9060 XT is just behind the RTX 5060 Ti, close enough to call it comparable. This trend does not continue at 1440p as the 8GB becomes a performance limiter in some titles. Ray tracing enthusiasts will be happy with AMD's progress on this front, but NVIDIA still wins in this arena.

name: NZXT C1000 Gold Core
text:
The NZXT C1000 Gold Core is a fully modular ATX 3.1 power supply with an additional hybrid fan mode and a 7 year warranty.

The C1000 Gold Core comes packaged with all of the basics that you'll need and nothing extra. The cable bag will be convenient for keeping track of the extra cables.

Compared to the

C750 Gold Core

, the C1000 Gold Core has additional cables. When compared to the

C850 Gold Core

, the C1000 Gold Core only has additional output power. The 600 W 12V-2x6 cable and 1000 W output capacity equip this power supply for all but the most power hungry single card systems.

The power supply enclosure is a basic stamped metal shell, but it feels sturdy and the internals show care and attention to detail.

The power supply omits the optional -12V rail, so that was not tested as part of our suite.

The C1000 Gold Core features a second fan mode, allowing you to choose between the fan always operating, and it stopping under light loads. This is controlled with a physical toggle switch on the rear of the chassis.

The power supply is equipped with all of the expected protections and it survived all of our onerous protections testing. We found that it will shut down before it causes damage to your equipment, or itself. When loading all rails together it shut down(presumably from OPP) at 126% of the rated load.

The output voltage ripple of the 12V rail is slightly higher than other more expensive options of this power capacity, but it is still well within the specifications and should cause no issues with the equipment that this power supply is capable of powering. It performed very similarly to the

C750 Gold Core

and

C850 Gold Core

.

The C1000 Gold Core also performs well in when testing output voltage regulation. We measured the output voltage of the C1000 Gold Core at around 12.20 V when under 2% load, allowing more voltage drop as the load increases without dipping below 12 V. It is not uncommon that power supplies will bias their output higher than nominal to account for voltage drop with increasing load.

As a result of the slightly higher 12V rail voltage the C1000 Gold Core was able to sustain all of the excursions, and keep the voltages above the ATX minimum voltage limits. This power supply should have no issues dealing with the excursions of cards connected to the 600 W 12V-2x6 connector.

name: NZXT C850 Gold Core
text:
The NZXT C850 Gold Core is a fully modular ATX 3.1 power supply with an additional hybrid fan mode and a 7 year warranty.

The C850 Gold Core comes packaged with all of the basics that you'll need and nothing extra. The cable bag will be convenient for keeping track of the extra cables.

The main difference from the

C750 Gold Core

is the included cables. While the C750 Gold Core comes with two PCIe 6+2 pin cables and a 300 W 12V-2x6 cable, the C850 Gold Core supplies three PCIe 6+2 pin cables and a 600 W 12V-2x6 cable. There should be plenty of connections for a variety of computer configurations.

While an 850 W power supply is on the low end of recommended power for a top-of-the-line card, this will still be able to power it for reasonable/moderate loads. If instead you have multiple lower power cards, this has the connections facilities to suffice.

The power supply enclosure is a basic stamped metal shell, but it feels sturdy and the internals show care and attention to detail.  The power supply omits the optional -12V rail, so that was not tested as part of our suite.

The C850 Gold Core features a second fan mode, allowing you to choose between the fan always operating, and it stopping under light loads. This is controlled with a physical toggle switch on the rear of the chassis.

The power supply is equipped with all of the expected protections and it survived all of our onerous protections testing. We found that it will shut down before it causes damage to your equipment, or itself. When loading all rails together it shut down(presumably from OPP) at 127% of the rated load.

The output voltage ripple of the 12V rail is slightly higher than other more expensive options of this power capacity, but it is still well within the specifications and should cause no issues with the equipment that this power supply is capable of powering. It performed very similarly to the C750 Gold Core.

The C850 Gold Core also performs well in when testing output voltage regulation. We measured the output voltage of the C850 Gold Core at around 12.14 V when under 2% load, allowing more voltage drop as the load increases without dipping below 12 V. It is not uncommon that power supplies will bias their output higher than nominal to account for voltage drop with increasing load.

As a result of the slightly higher 12V rail voltage the C850 Gold Core was able to sustain all of the excursions, and keep the voltages above the ATX minimum voltage limits. This power supply should have no issues dealing with the excursions of cards connected to the 600 W 12V-2x6 connector.

name: ASUS PRIME GeForce RTX 5060 Ti 16GB
text:
The RTX 5060 Ti 16 GB is the least expensive option to get 16 GB on a Blackwell graphics card.

The 5060 TI is a fully enabled GB206 Blackwell die with 4608 CUDA cores, 144 Tensor cores, and 36 Ray Tracing cores. This PRIME model from ASUS is a triple fan design with a boost clock up to 2602 MHz. The RTX 5060 Ti has a relatively small 128-bit bus, but manages a memory bandwidth of 448 GB/s thanks to the speedy GDDR7.

The RTX 5060 Ti has a 180 W TDP and a single 6+2-pin PCIe power connection. There are smaller two-fan variants of the RTX 5060 Ti, but in this case the triple fans keep this card cool while under load. This is an oversized cooling solution as the smaller versions are plenty enough cooling for this graphics processor.

The GeForce RTX 5060 Ti supports DirectX 12 Ultimate and NVIDIA G-SYNC, enabling a smooth and responsive gaming experiences. It also integrates NVIDIA's DLSS 4 Multi Frame Generation. DLSS 4 features like Super Resolution and Ray Reconstruction improve image quality and scaling, however, older GPUs may see performance drops. Multi Frame Gen is exclusive to the RTX 50 series, generating up to 3 frames for every rendered frame with low latency impact, as long as your base frame rate is enough.

Encoding and decoding capabilities are robust, with the 9th Generation Encode and 6th Generation Decoder supporting H.264, H.265/HEVC, and AV1 formats. Additionally, the Blackwell architecture introduces support for 4:2:2 chroma-sampled video encoding and decoding in H.264 and H.265 formats, a feature highly beneficial for professional workflows.

At 1080p the RTX 5060 Ti is a league away from the of an RTX 5070, reminiscent of the performance gulf between the RTX 4060 Ti and RTX 4070. The RX 9060 XT is nearly identical in overall 1080p and 1440p performance which is a good showing from AMD. 

NVIDIA retains dominance in ray tracing performance, but that lead is getting smaller with every generation.

name: Flux Flux Keyboard
text:
The Flux Keyboard is a hyper-customizable keyboard merging a macropad into your main interface device.

The baseline configuration comes with the keyboard itself, two cables, a keyframe of your choice, and the passive module kit.  Purchasing the keyboard from their website walks you through a configurator where additional modules and keyframes can be selected.

The 'keyframe' is the main acrylic section of the keyboard, housing all of the keys in the 75% layout.  Each keyboard will come in your selection of an ANSI or ISO layout, with either tactile or linear keys.  Additional keyframes can be purchased for simple reconfiguration of the keyboard.  The passive module kit simply presents ‘windows’ for the display to show any of the customizable widgets.  We weren’t able to test any of the knob or button modules as they will be shipping September 2026, but they will provide additional controls and features.

The aluminum chassis feels premium and weighs in at a whopping 2.2 kg.  You do get a dedicated function row along with the modular section, making this keyboard deeper than most.

The keyboard comes with a USB Type-C to Type-C cable, as well as a USB Type-C to USB Type-C/USB Type-A Y-splitter cable.  This is because it has a power draw of up to 15 W as reported by the manufacturer, with a minimum of 7.5 W.  Some devices may not be able to drive this, so you’re able to connect to your computer via USB-C while powering the keyboard with the USB-A connection.  We performed some ad hoc power testing in a typical configuration and found that it was drawing roughly 7 W on average, with regular spikes to 8 W.  There may be more stressing configurations that do bring it up near the 15 W draw, but this shouldn’t be an issue for most desktop computers.

We were able to confirm the claimed display brightness of 300 nits, measuring 326 nits on a white background.  Placing the acrylic keyframe over the display reduced the measured luminance to around 280 nits.  That is fairly standard brightness for computer monitors and we found it bright enough to use in most environments, though there can be some dimming at oblique angles.  We took some photographs with the production lights off to better represent the viewing experience.

The included

Flux Polymath

software is still in “early access testing” at the time of writing this.  It allows for control of the key profiles, mapping, and display.  However, it doesn’t currently support macro programming like you see in other programmable keyboard software. Flux Polymath does allow for some convenient adjustments like key parallax adjustment and individual actuation distance, since this is a Hall effect keyboard.

The captures in the software section illustrate the many customization features available.  We found the software relatively straightforward to use despite the many fields, though this is an evolving program so future quality and included features cannot be evaluated or assumed.

With the full keyboard display and additional modules, performance clearly isn’t the primary goal of this keyboard, though it can hold its own when used for normal computing tasks.  Many of our keystroke fields are blank as this is quite an abnormal keyboard and we didn't perform key actuation testing on it.

The keyboard does have a 1000 Hz polling rate like many gaming keyboards, but we tested the latency to be around 10 ms over the wired connection, the only mode it can operate in.  This was conducted at the default 1.2 mm actuation distance and is a perfectly acceptable result for a productivity keyboard. While 10 ms is not

terrible

for a gaming keyboard, it’s just not competitive with other players in the space, typically measuring below 6 ms in our experience.

name: Lian Li RS1000G
text:
The Lian Li RS1000G 1000 W is an ATX 3.1 power supply with an MSRP of $159.99 USD and many qualities beyond the titular "Rotational Shift"(RS).

This power supply comes with an entire suite of extras. There are cable management accessories, supporting hardware for the USB hub, and cover plates that can be installed for either rotational configuration. The provided cables are very flexible; combined with the customizability of the power supply direction, there should be no issues with cable management.

The rotating power plug feels sturdy and I don't suspect that it will sustain any damage with the limited number of times you'll likely be switching it. The implementation is as 'simple' as ensuring there is extra slack for the internal connections and that they won't be pinched when rotating.

Lian Li adds extra polish to the build by including cosmetic cover plates for the 'unused' sides. Beyond looking nice, these can direct airflow and prevent unintentional rotation of the AC plug.

The most notable accessory is the RS Hub which can be mounted on the power supply just above the connection points for the output power cables. The RS Hub is a USB hub that clips onto the power supply or can be magnetically affixed elsewhere in your case, providing a convenient connection point for RGB and USB accessories.

There is a single hybrid mode for the 135 mm Fluid Dynamic Bearing fan; it will not turn on until the power supply exceeds an approximately 25% load, then will remain on as cooling is needed.  

During our Over Power Protection(OPP) testing we found that the power supply always shut down around 120% of the maximum rated load, very similar to the limit found in the 12 V Over Current Protection(OCP) testing. Beyond the six protections we check for, they also claim No Load Protection(NLP) and Surge & Inrush Protection(SIP). These are essential features to have in a consumer power supply, but they're so essential that they're assumed to be present, and often are not advertised.

The ripple voltage that we measured on this power supply is in line with other 1000 W power supplies we've tested. We did experience some slightly elevated noise in the 50-90% load range at 230 V input, but it is completely within specifications and predominantly on the secondary rails so it will cause no issues in regular use.

Voltage regulation across the load range and in excursion testing was very good. All output rails maintained steady outputs up to 100% load and there was only a single excursion test at 0°C where it failed to sustain the power spike. The power supply sustained the same level of excursion(100 ms) at other temperatures and input voltages, so this is unlikely to be a common occurrence.

name: NZXT C750 Gold Core
text:
The NZXT C750 Gold Core is a fully modular ATX 3.1 power supply with an additional hybrid fan mode and a 7 year warranty.

The C750 Gold Core comes packaged with all of the basics that you'll need and nothing extra. The cable bag will be convenient for keeping track of the extra cables, but with only two total PCIe power connections you may find them lacking if your GPU isn't powered over 12V-2x6. The cables omit the sleeving of the C Gold series, but are still very flexible and easy to use.

The 12V-2x6 cable is only rated for 300 W. This is sensible for a power supply of this capacity, but some other brands do include a 600 W cable at this power supply tier.

The power supply enclosure is a basic stamped metal shell, but it feels sturdy and the internals show care and attention to detail.

The power supply omits the optional -12V rail, so that was not tested as part of our suite.

The C750 Gold Core features a second fan mode, allowing you to choose between the fan always operating, and it stopping under light loads. This is controlled with a physical toggle switch on the rear of the chassis.

The power supply is equipped with all of the expected protections and it survived all of our onerous protections testing. We found that it will shut down before it causes damage to your equipment, or itself. When loading all rails together it shut down(presumably from OPP) at 133% of the rated load.

The output voltage ripple of the 12V rail is slightly higher than other more expensive options of this power capacity, but it is still well within the specifications and should cause no issues with the equipment that this power supply is capable of powering.

The voltage regulation as the output load increases is a similar story, there are better options if the load regulation is critical, but this power supply still performs in the 'typical' range and well within the required range. The 12V rail drops roughly 200 mV from an unloaded condition to a full load, reaching a minimum of 11.791 V.

The C750 Gold Core performed well in the excursion testing, sustaining all of the excursions, from 0.1 ms to 100 ms, and the 12V rail only minorly dropped below the specification. It will be able to handle any GPU connected through the 300 W 12V-2x6 cable.

name: ASUS PRIME GeForce RTX 5060
text:
The RTX 5060 is the most anticipated model for value from NVIDIA's Blackwell generation of video cards.

The RTX 5060 has the same GB206 Blackwell die as its bigger brother RTX 5060 Ti, though with a few less cores enabled. This PRIME model from ASUS is a triple fan design with a boost clock up to 2527 MHz. The RTX 5060 has a relatively small 128-bit bus, but manages a memory bandwidth of 448 GB/s thanks to the speedy GDDR7.

The RTX 5060 has a tame 145 W TDP and takes a a single 6+2-pin PCIe connector. The cooling solution is nearly twice the size of the PCB and we don't think it needs to be so large, resulting  in an annoyingly large card for a 60 series. There are RTX 5060 low profile and single fan options so we aren't entirely sure what possessed AIB's to produce such behemoths at the low end.

The GeForce RTX 5060 supports DirectX 12 Ultimate and NVIDIA G-SYNC, enabling a smooth and responsive gaming experiences. It also integrates NVIDIA's DLSS 4 Multi Frame Generation. DLSS 4 features like Super Resolution and Ray Reconstruction improve image quality and scaling, however, older GPUs may see performance drops. Multi Frame Gen is exclusive to the RTX 50 series, generating up to 3 frames for every rendered frame with low latency impact, as long as your base frame rate is enough.

Encoding and decoding capabilities are robust, with the 9th Generation Encode and 6th Generation Decoder supporting H.264, H.265/HEVC, and AV1 formats. Additionally, the Blackwell architecture introduces support for 4:2:2 chroma-sampled video encoding and decoding in H.264 and H.265 formats, a feature highly beneficial for professional workflows.

At 1080p and 1440p the RTX 5060 is just slightly behind its AMD rival the RX 9060 XT, but is ahead of the previous generation's RTX 4060 Ti. NVIDIA separates themselves in ray tracing performance, but this generation AMD isn't far behind. If you aren't looking for the CUDA advantage in a particular task (AI, Creator, etc...) you may want to look at the Arc B580 and RX 9060 XT for best-bang-for-the-buck-and-more-vram.

name: Colorful iGame GeForce RTX 5070 Mini OC
text:
A small but no-compromises RTX 5070 model fit for the discerning small form factor enthusiast.

The RTX 5070 is powered by a Blackwell GB205 processor with 6144 CUDA Cores, 192 Tensor cores, and 48 Ray Tracing cores. The RTX 5070 has 37% fewer CUDA Cores than the next tier, the RTX 5070 Ti, but only about 20% less performance. The 5070 Ti does get a bump to 16 GB of VRAM which gives it an edge over the RTX 5070 for workloads that require that such as 4K gaming and AI inference.  This graphics card has the same GDDR7 memory and PCIe Gen 5.0 connection found on the rest of the Blackwell lineup (minus the anemic RTX 5050). 

Despite its smaller dimensions the iGame RTX 5070 Mini has some heft to it, our model coming in at 818.57 g. On this tier of card and below we would welcome a departure from the 12V-2x6 connector, but  it seems evident NVIDIA is content on pushing this connector for a few generations yet.

Blackwell GPUs support DirectX 12 Ultimate and NVIDIA G-SYNC, enabling smooth and responsive gaming experiences. It also integrates NVIDIA's DLSS 4 Multi Frame Generation. DLSS 4 features like Super Resolution and Ray Reconstruction improve image quality and scaling, however older GPUs may see performance drops. Multi Frame Gen is exclusive to the RTX 50 series, generating up to 3 frames for every rendered frame.  This is designed to have low latency impact, as long as your base frame rate meets a baseline playable threshold.

Encoding and decoding capabilities are robust, with the 9th Generation Encode and 6th Generation Decoder supporting H.264, H.265/HEVC, and AV1 formats. Additionally, the Blackwell architecture introduces support for 4:2:2 chroma-sampled video encoding and decoding in H.264 and H.265 formats, a greatly beneficial feature for professional workflows.

The iGame RTX 5070 Mini exhibits identical performance to the Founder's Edition, and is even cooler in most of our workloads. The RTX 5070 positions itself well in performance over the RTX 5060 Ti 16 GB, but if you are after VRAM capacity, it's more economical to drop down to the RTX 5060 Ti 16 GB instead of going up a tier to the 5070 Ti 16 GB.

name: Sapphire PULSE Radeon RX 9070
text:
The RX 9070 fits in AMD's lineup between the RX9060 XT, and the RX 9070 XT above it, launching at a welcome $549 USD in late Q1 2025.

The RX 9070's heart is a variant of the Navi 48 processor manufactured with TSMC's N4P process node. This graphics card comes with a PCIe 5.0 x16 interface and 256-bit memory bus with its GDDR6 memory rated for 640 GB/s memory bandwidth. Unlike NVIDIA's use of GDDR7 on the 5000 series, the RX 9070 is equipped with GDDR6. 

The RX 9070 is 8 compute units short of a fully enabled Navi 48 processor coming in at 3584 streaming multiprocessors, 56 ray tracing units, and 112 AI accelerators.  It boosts to a respectable 2520 MHz, 450 MHz less than the RX 9070 XT, but also maintains a much tamer power envelope with a TDP of 220 W. We did see transient spikes as high as 321 W, but most modern power supplies(ATX 3) will handle these spikes without issue. Thermals on this particular card are also well in hand with the dual fans keeping it under 60°C in our MSI Kombustor stress test.

AMD FidelityFX Super Resolution 4 (FSR4) is now available, but only on 9000 series (RDNA4) cards. It provides a notable quality uplift in the games that support it. 

Video encoding has improved over last generation, and it encodes in true 1920x1080,

not 1920x1082

. This generation does not support 10-bit 4:2:2 encoding so that may make this card less desirable for video professionals, but it does have an improved quality of encoding at lower bitrates.

At 1080p the RX 9070 lands right next to last generations RX 7900 GRE and slightly ahead of the NVIDIA RTX 5070. Increasing the resolution to 1440p does not change its position on the chart, but its nice to see its geomean 1% lows are a healthy 81 FPS. We test at maximum graphics settings so our results are close to "worst case" performance you can expect in the games we tested. Without FSR enabled its hard to say this is a 4K capable graphics card, but as this is the worst it can be, you could get a decent 4K experience at the expense of tactically lowering some of your settings.

AMD hasn't quite caught up to NVIDIA in ray tracing performance, but their implementation is respectable enough to be called usable, even beating out the previous flagship RX 7900 XTX.

name: MSI MAG A650BN
text:
The MSI MAG A650BN is a permanently connected power supply with minimal accessories and acceptable performance in our testing, except for suspect results handling brownouts.

This power supply is packaged with truly the bare minimum of accessories. It only has the AC cable, documentation, and mounting bolts for installation in your case. It doesn't come with any additional cables as they're all permanently attached. It is difficult to expect much more when considering the low MSRP. Our testing did confirm the claimed 80PLUS Bronze rating of the power supply.

As with the MAG A750BN PCIe 5 model, the efficiency below 50% load exceeded the claimed 80PLUS Bronze values by a considerable amount, over 3% at a 20% load.

The MAG A650BN is advertised with all of the standard protections except UVP. This feature is designed to protect your computer from dropping below ATX specifications and causing

undefined behaviour

.  The power supply performed well in our Over Power Protection(OPP) testing, disengaging just above 130%.  This is a reasonable margin above the rated output to allow for power spikes while also triggering if there is a fault.

However, we observed that at wattages far exceeding the output rating, the 12V rail dropped to 10.5 V and 9.6 V before the power supply shut down.  This is unlikely to affect regular use of the power supply, but it is evidence of a lack of UVP.

While all of our voltage ripple/noise measurements remain within the ATX specification, they're not particularly impressive. The 12V rail, the most important for low noise operation, ramps up quite steeply as the output wattage is increased. This may present stability issues if you are using this power supply in a system with high power components and operating near the upper limit.

Load regulation performance is solid across the rails and this is mirrored with the excursion results. This is notable as the power supply is not advertised to be compliant with ATX 3.0(or 3.1), and is not expected to meet the same requirements. We conducted the reduced level of excursion testing for power supplies without a 16-pin connector, and the MAG A650BN performed quite well, only failing to sustain a few 100ms excursions at higher temperatures.

name: Razer Boomslang 20th Anniversary Edition
text:
A nostalgic collector focused remake of the original Boomslang that pairs premium materials and strong wireless performance with divisive ergonomics.

While it ships with PTFE skates pre-installed, glass skates are also included in the box. A display case with the ‘exploded’ components of the mouse (separate from the actual mouse that you would use) is included, which makes sense given the premium pricing and limited run. The display case also lights up using a USB Type-A to USB Type-C cable. It genuinely feels like a collector package rather than a standard retail product.

The PU leather sections feel soft and premium. The glossy plastic sections, however, are very prone to fingerprints and smudging. It looks fantastic when clean, but will likely require maintenance to stay that way. Construction quality is excellent. The mouse and the included wireless charging stand both feel sturdy and well assembled with no creaks or flex.

Connectivity is unconventional. There are no ports on the mouse itself. The 2.4 GHz dongle is integrated directly into the wireless charging stand which is neat but a little gimmicky over functionality, and there is no Bluetooth support. So the only way it works is when you wire up the stand to your computer and use the mouse wirelessly. The cable for the charging stand is 185 cm long. This should be long enough for most use cases.

The companion software is surprisingly robust. Independent X and Y DPI adjustment is available, which is uncommon and useful for niche sensitivity tuning. DPI can be adjusted in increments of 1 DPI, allowing extremely granular sensitivity control.

There is a toggle to disable mouse lighting when your monitor is off. It is a small but thoughtful touch for users who care about desk aesthetics or power saving. Automatic power saving behaviors can be configured based on usage state and battery level.

A smart polling rate switcher allows the mouse to automatically jump to 8000 Hz in fullscreen applications and revert to a lower polling rate otherwise. This helps preserve battery life while still enabling maximum responsiveness during gameplay. This is an excellent feature we have not seen in many mice. All of these features are plug and play with no additional windows configurations required which is quite nice.

There is a macOS version of the app, the mouse itself works on macOS but the mouse is not

supported

by the app. Consequently, we were not able to configure any of the features (mentioned above) on a macOS device. Poetically, the original Boomslang mouse also did not work well on macOS. In theory, Razer could add support for the mouse in the macOS software in the future.

Wireless 2.4 GHz (via the included charging stand) click latency measured under 2 ms. That places it comfortably in the competitive tier. While not sub 1 ms bleeding edge, the difference would be imperceptible in real world use. Tracking is consistent across the DPI range tested. No obvious jitter, spin outs, or tracking anomalies were observed. Performance is really solid and reliable.

Primary buttons are loud with a noticeable high pitched chassis ping which we would not call desirable. Side buttons and the scroll wheel sound and feel average. This can be heard in the sound sample video.

name: NVIDIA RTX Pro 6000 Blackwell Workstation Edition
text:
The NVIDIA RTX Pro 6000 Blackwell Workstation edition is the fastest desktop workstation card from NVIDIA.

The RTX Pro 6000 sports a GB202 processor, the same GPU used in the RTX 5090. A full fat GB202 features 12 graphics processing clusters, each containing 16 streaming multiprocessors, with each SM packing 128 CUDA cores. To create the GB202 variants used in the RTX 5090 and RTX Pro 6000, NVIDIA appears to be disabling individual streaming multiprocessors rather than entire clusters. The RTX Pro 6000 is only missing four SMs from a fully enabled GB202. Whether those extra 512 CUDA cores are being saved for a future RTX 5090 Ti Super X or simply reflect yield realities remains to be seen.

The RTX Pro 6000 Blackwell Workstation Edition shares nearly the same cooling design as the RTX 5090, using a dual flow-through cooler with a compact PCB centered between the fans. One notable difference is that while the RTX 5090 uses liquid metal on the die, the RTX Pro 6000 does not.

Boost clocks are also higher on the RTX Pro 6000, increasing from 2.41 GHz on the RTX 5090 to 2.61 GHz here. Out-of-box power consumption climbs accordingly, from 575 W to 600 W. The card retains the same 512-bit memory bus, memory speed, and bandwidth as the RTX 5090, but the memory configuration is where things get absurd. The RTX Pro 6000 carries 96GB of GDDR7 with ECC support, which GDDR7 enables without a reported performance penalty. This is accomplished using 32 3GB memory modules, with 16 mounted on each side of the PCB.

The RTX Pro 6000 is restricted to RTX Pro or Quadro desktop drivers. The exact practical differences between these and standard GeForce drivers are not always clear, but depending on how gaming features are rolled out, this could be a significant downside if you were considering this card for gaming alongside professional workloads.

At 1080p, both the RTX Pro 6000 and RTX 5090 are heavily CPU bottlenecked and often deliver identical performance. In several games, the RTX 5090 has been observed clocking slightly higher and pulling ahead, likely due to driver behavior or the additional power overhead associated with the Pro card’s massive VRAM.

At 1440p, the RTX Pro 6000 begins to separate itself, with its roughly 10% higher core count starting to matter. 

At 4K, the gap becomes much more consistent and measurable, with some titles seeing performance gains that just break into the double digits.

name: Cooler Master MM710
text:
At around $50 USD, the Cooler Master MM710 is a comfortable and well built lightweight wired mouse that is held back almost entirely by unusually poor latency performance.

The box contents are extremely minimal. You get the mouse itself and basic documentation, with no extras or accessories included. The MM710 uses an ABS honeycomb chassis with a glossy coating. Despite the aggressive cutouts, the mouse feels sturdy with very little flex under normal grip pressure. Overall construction feels solid and functional but not premium per se.

Cooler Master MasterPlus+

(like C++, the name is not redundant at all) is straightforward and easy to use. The interface is clear, and common tasks such as button remapping, DPI adjustment, and macro creation are quick to access. No mandatory account or registration is required.

Despite being a wired mouse running at a 1000 Hz polling rate, the MM710 consistently showed very high latency in our testing. Multiple runs were conducted, and results never improved beyond an average of roughly 24.5 ms, which is well outside what we would expect from a wired gaming mouse. While the latency wasn’t exactly erratic, it is still objectively poor and difficult to ignore, especially for competitive or latency sensitive use. Outside of latency, performance is otherwise unremarkable. Tracking from the PMW3389 sensor was stable with no obvious issues and button response felt crisp and consistent.

name: Aula H530
text:
A visually distinctive wireless mouse with a novelty spinner feature, let down by poor latency, inconsistent sensor performance, and a complete lack of software support.

The Aula H530 ships with a USB Type-A wireless receiver, and user documentation. There are no extra accessories, replacement feet, or cables included. Build quality is decent overall. The ABS chassis feels sturdy and the mouse has a substantial weight that gives it a solid presence during use. The matte coating feels fine in hand and the PTFE feet glide smoothly. The overall construction does not feel cheap, but it also does not stand out as particularly premium beyond its “design”.

There is no companion software for the Aula H530. AULA states in the manual that the mouse should be able to pair with a SteelSeries Rival 3 Wireless receiver and be configured through SteelSeries Engine software. In practice, we could not get this to work. During testing, the mouse could not be discovered or customized through SteelSeries software in any configuration.

As a result, there are no customization options available to us at all. DPI levels, button functions, polling rate, and lighting could not be adjusted. There is also no onboard memory, cloud sync, or macro support. The fidget spinner integrated into the top shell is the only unique feature of note, and while it is visually interesting, it does not meaningfully improve usability.

Latency performance is well below what we would expect from a modern wireless mouse. Using the 2.4 GHz receiver, the mouse averaged 33.24 ms. Over Bluetooth, latency increased further with a mean of 49.95 ms.  This is significantly higher than the roughly 10 ms baseline we consider average for wired or wireless gaming mice, and the added delay is very noticeable in use.

DPI behavior is inconsistent. While the mouse advertises a resolution range of 800 to 4800 DPI, tracking accuracy varies depending on test length. In short movement tests around 25.4 mm, the sensor behaves consistently but tracks well below its stated sensitivity. In longer movement tests around 880 mm, the sensor struggles significantly and fails to register a large portion of input. This results in unreliable cursor movement and will make precise aiming difficult.

Click sound and feel are a strong point. The main buttons are crisp and clicky, with good tactility and consistent actuation across the length of the buttons. The side buttons are softer but still responsive, and overall button feel is one of the better aspects of the mouse.

name: Glorious Model O 2
text:
At $99.99 USD, the Glorious Model O 2 Wireless offers excellent wired latency, solid build quality, and comfortable ergonomics, but its higher than expected wireless latency and some usability quirks keeps it from being a top tier competitive wireless option.

The mouse ships with the expected accessories for a modern wireless gaming mouse. All included items are of decent quality and feel appropriate for the product. Build quality is solid with no rattles or flex during use. The matte plastic finish provides some grip and feels pleasant in hand, though users with sweaty hands may notice some slip. The placement for the main and the side buttons should feel usable for most people. Overall, the mouse feels sturdy and well assembled.

The Glorious Model O 2 Wireless uses

Glorious Core software

for configuration, which covers all the expected basics without doing anything unusual. The software itself is easy to use and straightforward, though it does require a local download and installation, which may be a minor annoyance for some users who have gotten used to the web apps that most companies provide nowadays. There is no cloud sync or multi device Bluetooth pairing support, and Glorious largely sticks to standard feature sets here rather than experimenting with novel or gimmicky additions.

Wired, the Model O 2 feels great. It is consistently responsive and never feels like it is getting in the way, which matches the strong wired latency results. Wireless is usable, but it is clearly slower, and the inconsistency shows up enough that you would notice it if you are sensitive to input feel or switching from a faster mouse. Bluetooth behaves exactly like Bluetooth and is fine for casual use only.

Tracking is solid at normal sensitivity ranges and never felt unreliable. At very high DPI it starts to feel a bit loose, but that is not where most people will run this mouse anyway. Clicks are a strong point, with snappy main buttons and side buttons that are softer but still easy to use without misclicks.

name: FSP Group Hydro PTM X Pro ATX 3.0 1200W
text:
A compact full ATX 1200W power supply offering good build quality and cable variety, with generally acceptable performance but few standout results for the price.

The power supply arrived with good packaging and all the expected accessories. There was also a PSU Tester and some stickers which are nice additions. The construction is decent with a wire fan grille which looks clean but it could be susceptible to long thin items falling in. 

The included ribbon cables are of good, usable quality and offer excellent modularity due to the wide variety of cables provided. The power supply's short depth, despite the stiff cables, makes it suitable for compact, high-power builds. The inclusion of a sleeved 12VHPWR cable is a smart choice, as it is often the most visible cable in a PC build. While it is a small nitpick, it is notable that the 12VHPWR cable was unlabelled. It is most likely a 600W cable and we assumed so for our tests.

The power ratings list a combined 18.6 W limit shared between the 5Vsb and -12V rails, rather than separate limits for each rail as is typical. We split these rails during testing and display to remain within the manufacturer’s stated combined rating. It is 80PLUS Platinum and Cybenetics ETA Platinum rated for efficiency.

The unit offers a 10-year warranty and it has two fan operating modes via a rear switch. In standard mode, the fan runs continuously. With ECO mode enabled, the fan remains off at low loads and does not begin spinning until 30% load, operating as a semi fanless hybrid mode.

According to the manufacturer’s specifications, the power supply lacks undervoltage protection (UVP), which is typically intended to safely shut down the power supply if output voltages fall too far below specification.  Despite the lack of stated UVP, we saw no issues during our brownout testing.

Ripple performance varied substantially between the two samples we tested. On the first unit, ripple at 115V input was substantially higher than at 230V, with all rails increasing sharply. The 12V rail remained within ATX limits due to the wider tolerance allowed by the specification, but the minor rails exceeded their 50 mV limits.

The second sample, which is the basis for the data shown on this page and on PSUCircuit, behaved far more normally. Ripple at both 115V and 230V inputs were slightly above average. Load regulation was somewhat weaker than expected for a power supply in this class. The 12V rail briefly exceeded ATX limits by a small margin during the excursion tests, but the unit was able to sustain all applied excursions without shutting down.

The 12V rail showed more variation under load than we typically see from comparable high end units, and the minor rails also dropped slightly more than expected, though all remained within ATX specification. While still within specifications, the 5Vsb particularly dropped quite a bit. Though, it is unlikely to affect everyday use for most people.

name: Super Flower Zillion FG Gold 1050W ATX 3.1
text:
A compact and flexible 1050 W ATX 3.1 power supply with good efficiency and strong brownout performance, but one that ultimately failed before completing our full test suite.

The Zillion FG Gold 1050W arrives with all the expected accessories. The PSU itself has solid construction, and the patterned fan grille should stop unwanted debris without restricting airflow. The effective length(the length of the power supply with cables plugged in) of the unit is slightly shorter than many ATX units thanks to the less common flat ribbon-style cables.

The flat ribbon-style cables are notably high quality and flexible. This should help quite a bit with cable management. Fan control is handled through an eco mode switch on the rear of the unit. Overall, the hardware experience is clean and practical, with no drawbacks.

The Zillion FG Gold 1050W includes two fan modes. There is a fan always operating mode where the fan speed changes dynamically as the load changes but the fan is always on. The ECO Mode starts with the fan off until a certain load percentage is reached and then adjusts the fan speed based on temperature and load. The ECO switch is located on the rear of the power supply. The unit supports the standard set of protections expected from an ATX 3.1 power supply, except Under Voltage Protection (UVP). Additionally, No Load Protection (NLP) is listed as one of the protections. 

It is odd to list NLP as a protection as it is basic functionality assumed of nearly all consumer power supplies. It means that the unit can function without faults even when there is no load connected. This can be important for larger and or niche power supplies that may become unstable with no load, but it is an assumed feature for any consumer product. 

These protections cover typical operating conditions, though the PSU’s behaviour under OCP load resulted in a failure during testing, which is discussed in the productivity and efficiency section.

Ripple performance is acceptable, but there is a clear difference between 115 V and 230 V input. On 115 V, noise on the 12V rail ramps up steeply as load increases, reaching relatively high values near full load. On 230 V, the ripple remains much lower and more stable across the entire sweep. All measurements still fall within ATX specifications, 115 V input causes significantly more output ripple.

The 12V rail and the -12V only drift by about 1% across the entire load range. The 5V and 3.3V rails follow a gentle downward trend with no instability, and different temperatures we tested at had little effect on the result.  The power supply failed to sustain a couple of the excursions with 100 ms duration and the 12V rail dipped below ATX voltage specifications for many of the excursions of shorter duration.  The 12V rail dips were minor and are unlikely to affect normal performance, but the excursion failures could result in your computer shutting down under extremely heavy loads.

name: Attack Shark R11 Ultra
text:
A well rounded wireless gaming mouse with a distinctive coating and strong latency performance at around $93 USD, even with its unpolished web software features.

The mouse doesn’t break the mold, being packaged with the standard gaming mouse loadout, but the quality of the accessories is solid. The included stickers are of the “attack shark” (presumably) that looks like Jeff the Landshark,

although it isn’t

. The relatively huge dongle and its bold design are also quite distinctive. The receiver is thoughtfully designed for gamers to reduce the need for an additional component: a USB extender. Instead, the usual USB Type-A to Type-C cable can be plugged into the receiver using the Type-C side and the Type-A side can be plugged into the computer. It might restrict portability though.

Build quality of the mouse itself is quite solid. Attack Shark uses a carbon fibre composite for its chassis material and it feels quite good in hand. The excellent feel of the mouse is also due to the “Nano-Metal Ice” coating that has stylized patterns reminiscent of lenticular holograms. It looks interesting and gives the mouse a distinct identity, but the textured surface may be prone to trapping dust or debris over time.

The R11 Ultra uses the

Attack Shark Mouse Hub

, which is a Windows only utility that handles the basics. There is no mandatory account registration, profiles save to onboard memory, and customization options like DPI stages, polling rate selection, button remapping, and macro programming all work as expected. 

The desktop software is easy to navigate, but the web based version is less polished, showing colours that may not be accessibility-friendly unless switched to light mode. We are unsure if this was an isolated problem on our setup but still worth noting. This should be an easy fix for Attack Shark though.

Clicks are crisp with good feedback and are not overly loud. There is a slight chassis ping on left and right clicks but it's minimal. Side buttons feel similar to the main buttons. Wireless communication with the receiver is the best performing mode at just over 2 ms. Wired latency is slightly higher at around 3 ms but it is still effectively imperceptible. Bluetooth is much slower but it's acceptable. Overall, while not the best we have tested, it is definitely up there and holds its own.

name: Chilkey ND104
text:
A solid full size daily driver candidate with great build quality, feel and fun extras. Although at $219 USD, it asks slightly more than most people expect from a keyboard with a tiny built-in screen in late 2025.

The keyboard arrives with a surprising set of extras, including a small interchangeable tray for holding desk bits like binder clips, spare dampeners and screws, and a very sturdy silicone-sheathed cable with reinforced connectors.

Build quality is quite robust with an aluminum chassis, an FR4 plate, and plate mounted stabilizers that keep larger keys feeling consistent. The keycaps are Double-Shot PBT and the matte texture feels good to the touch, though it is a little strange that Chilkey did not use their own branded keycaps here.

The ND104 uses

a web based configurator

, which is great for avoiding extra downloads. Although, Chilkey does not unify their web-based software across models into one web-app. The ND75 uses an entirely different interface, as do

all of their other products

, which seems quite clunky.

Once you are in, basic remapping and macro programming work fine, but uploading images to the keyboard’s tiny screen is vague and picky about sizing, which makes changing the defaults more frustrating than it should be. VIA compatibility is a welcome surprise and helps offset the single onboard profile and lack of cloud sync, though it is not QMK firmware compatible, which is quite odd.

I am not sure who uses a calculator on their keyboard display but if you do, you likely won’t be using this one. It takes about 2 seconds for the inputs to show up on the display after you input them. Sometimes it even takes more than that - to the point that it becomes completely unusable. There is also an almighty control knob that can be reconfigured to a few different things.

RGB control is handled through the same configurator with per key lighting, a handful of solid presets, and brightness. The feature set feels complete on paper but the execution needs more polishing, especially for a keyboard that leans so heavily on fun extras like a built-in display.

Usually gimmicky keyboards do not test well but this one subverted our expectations. The keys were quite smooth and responsive. The auditory experience is excellent for a mainly aluminum keyboard. To our surprise there were no high pings when typing, rather there were only deep thocks. Latency performance was great across the board with wired averaging 3.99ms, the 2.4GHz receiver at 6.13ms, and Bluetooth a tad behind at 10.99ms. The solid performance across the board makes this an easy recommendation for people who like 100% layouts for their daily driver and don’t mind splurging a little.

name: NZXT C1200 Gold
text:
The NZXT C1200 Gold ATX 3.1 is a $209 USD fully modular power supply that would be the perfect recommendation for high wattage builds if not for 2 units failing our OCP test.

The NZXT C1200 Gold ATX 3.1 is an ATX form factor power supply. Like its lower wattage counterparts, the C1200 doesn’t come with many extras, but their quality is great. Given the user documentation is easily accessible on their product page, it is not missed in the box. We tested two units, one black and one white. Both variants come with matching black/white braided cables and connectors which is nice to see.

The construction feels premium, the design is minimal and the 135 mm fluid dynamic bearing fan is covered by a grill that is non-obstructive to airflow while blocking unwanted debris. NZXT also makes an impressive claim that the fan is completely silent when under 50% load. The braided cables are of great quality, although a potential drawback of the stiff braided cables is that they might be more difficult to manage into compact cases. Overall the hardware is what we expect from this NZXT series: it is great!

The C1200 Gold ATX 3.1 offers both a regular fan mode and a Zero Fan (hybrid) mode. In the Zero Fan Mode mode, the 135 mm FDB fan is designed to remain off until the PSU reaches roughly 50% load, allowing silent operation during light and moderate workloads. This mode can be enabled or disabled with a physical switch on the rear of the unit.

The PSU includes the standard set of protections and is backed by a 10-year warranty. Over Power Protection (OPP) behaved as expected, consistently activating around 134% of the rated load. However, both tested samples failed during the 12 V OCP test, where the PSU reached 134% of the rated load before shutting down and failing to recover. While the unit delivered otherwise solid performance, these protection failures prevent us from recommending it.

Overall performance is quite good. Voltage ripple is consistently low across all rails, though not exceptionally low, with the 12 V rail reaching 29 mV peak-to-peak at full load. The 5 V, 3.3 V, and 5 Vsb rails also remained well within ATX limits. There are no stability concerns here, and the results align with what we expect from a modern 80PLUS Gold power supply.


Load regulation is average for this wattage class. The 12 V rail shows a gradual reduction in output voltage as load increases, but it stayed above 12 V throughout the test at all temperatures tested. The minor rails trend slightly downward as well, though all remained within ATX specifications. It shouldn’t introduce any issues for most systems.

The unit passed most excursion durations across temperatures and input voltages, but the 12 V rail failed the shortest 0.1 ms and 1.0 ms tests. Where failures did occur, the dips fell just outside ATX voltage limits and never dropped below 11.1 V. This behaviour is unlikely to affect real-world workloads, though it is weaker than other solid performers in this class. We also observed some ringing during the 1.0 ms excursions at lower temperatures, which did not cause functional issues but it is worth noting.

Despite these shortcomings, the C1200 Gold performs reasonably well under steady-state and high-load conditions. The main limitation is its protections performance rather than these performances.

name: Gravastar Mercury V75 Pro Special Edition - Neon Graffiti

specs.rgbSoftware: Gravastar Magnetic Switch Keyboard Web Driver
specs.softwareName: Gravastar Magnetic Switch Keyboard Web Driver

text:
A flashy, magnetic 75% keyboard for a steep $260 that performs as fast as it looks loud, complete with sci-fi flair.

The keyboard makes an immediate impression with its loud, graffiti-style keycap design and neon chassis lighting. The printed legend can look cluttered at first glance, making them a little harder to read, but the matte keycaps are smooth to the touch. The board comes with a cleaning brush and cloth, thoughtful inclusions that fit the style and presentation.

With its full aluminum construction, it’s heavy at around 1.7 kg, keeping it stable on the desk. The distinct mechanical legs make it look like a small mech ready for battle, but they also increase its footprint and can snag on cables if you’re not careful. The V75 Pro is wired-only, with no Bluetooth or wireless modes available.

The most obvious “feature” of this keyboard is its loud look, which blends the styles of modern art and alien artifacts. Whether that’s a feature or a bug is up to you. The

Gravastar Magnetic Switch Keyboard Web Driver

is easy to use with no sign-ups or hidden menus, offering per-key RGB and chassis accent lighting that can be tuned to match the graffiti aesthetic. It also lets you adjust the magnetic switches for actuation depth and rapid reset. It supports four onboard profiles and macro programming. Out of box macOS compatibility along with the usual Windows compatibility is always nice to have too.

It uses Gateron Magnetic Jade linear switches, which are hot-swappable and offer a relatively short pre-travel (and travel) out of the box. Of course that can be changed with the web driver’s adjustable actuation. Although the actuation data did not look ideal they felt relatively smooth and the sound leaned toward “poppy” rather than deep which we prefer, but that’s personal taste. The likely cause is the carbon plate or the switch pad but it could also depend on the quality and amount of foam pad used. What isn’t subjective is the performance. An average latency of just 0.93 ms means this thing is undeniably fast.

name: Seasonic S12III 650W
text:
A budget 650 W power supply with basic performance and limited protections that failed during our own protections testing.

The power supply ships with minimal packaging that provides only basic protection. The enclosure is a simple stamped steel design with a standard layout and no unusual construction features. All cables are permanently attached, which limits flexibility during installation, but the main motherboard, CPU, and PCIe cables are flexible and use sleeved insulation that looks cleaner than the typical budget PVC (plastic) ones.

The unit uses a 120 mm sleeve bearing fan for cooling. Sleeve bearings are common in entry level models but generally have reduced longevity and produce more noise. Overall, the hardware is serviceable for basic systems but reflects the cost driven nature of the S12III series. The power ratings are pretty typical, however the input voltage range is a bit smaller than usual. It is only 110-240V input instead of the typical 100-240V. The test procedure was slightly altered to cater to this specification.

The S12III operates with a single always-on fan profile, so there is no hybrid or semi-passive mode available. This is expected for a budget platform but results in constant fan noise and airflow regardless of load.

The protection set listed by the manufacturer covers the OPP, SCP, OVP, and UVP functions. Seasoned readers will notice that OCP and OTP are omitted. Budget platforms often implement these with minimal margin, and our testing confirmed that while the power supply did shut down during high loads, the unit ultimately failed during the OCP portion of the test sequence. Subsequently, we cannot recommend it. 

OCP is particularly important because of what it does. In theory, if the power supply is supplying more current than the components can safely support then the OCP will interrupt the power, protecting you, the device, and anything connected to it.

Ripple performance is a concerning part of this unit. The 12V and 5V rail climb toward the upper limit of the ATX specification at high load, while the 3.3V, and 5Vsb rails slightly exceed the specification range near full load at 52mV and 54mV.

Voltage regulation is acceptable but loose. All positive rails remain within ATX limits, although each shows a noticeable downward trend as the load rises. The negative 12V rail deviates upward and does not track cleanly across the sweep, which is unusual but functionally non-problematic as most current systems do not use the -12V rail.

Excursion behaviour reflects the age of the platform. Since this is not an ATX 3.0 unit, it is not designed to pass the excursion tests, but we conducted them out of curiosity. It managed some of the reduced excursions we tested, but the longer one (100ms durations) caused the output rails to drop out. The unit has limited overhead when dealing with fast load changes relative to modern power supplies.

name: Logitech Pro X Superlight 2c
text:
The Pro X Superlight 2c refines Logitech’s flagship formula with a lighter, more compact body and flawless performance, but the build quality doesn’t quite live up to its $160 USD price tag.

The mouse comes with the usual accessories and some useful extras. Everything is neatly packed and feels premium enough for the price range, with the optional grip tape and replacement feet being nice inclusions for those who play competitively or want extended durability. However, the mouse comes installed with a bottom that has a blatant advertisement for the POWERPLAY charging pad that is not included instead of the bottom with glide skates that they do include in the box (what should have been installed in the first place). 

The construction is where the tradeoffs show. The chassis is matte-coated plastic and, while very lightweight, it flexes slightly under pressure. The weight reduction helps with glide and overall responsiveness, but it does lead to minor creaks and noticeable deflection near the scroll wheel when the main buttons are pressed firmly. PTFE feet ensure smooth movement, and the overall fit and finish are consistent with Logitech’s usual standards, but the build does feel less rigid than what you’d expect from a premium-tier mouse.

The 2c runs on

Logitech’s G-Hub

, which is quite extensive and kind of overwhelming at times. At least it means that there are a lot of customization settings. Up to five profiles can be saved directly on the mouse, so settings carry over between systems. Given the competitive audience that this mouse caters to, the absence of macro support and cloud sync (often more desired by productivity focused users) is not a huge deal. The mouse is also compatible with Logitech’s

POWERPLAY

wireless charging pad that can be purchased separately.

It uses Logitech’s own switches that they call “

optical-mechanical

”. As far as we can tell, it is an optical switch but it has a more than average tactile feedback like a mechanical switch. The latency performance is excellent, averaging 1.31 ms in both wired and receiver modes. Even the maximum latency measurements stay well below 2 ms, placing it among the consistently fastest mice we have tested. The HERO 2 sensor tracks smoothly across all DPI levels with no jitter or acceleration issues.

name: Kinesis Freestyle Edge RGB
text:
A split mechanical keyboard with switches you might want to replace, per-key RGB, and an ergonomic design that should make long gaming sessions easier without feeling unfamiliar.

The Freestyle Edge RGB comes with only the documentation, though the detachable palm rests can be quite useful. The plastic chassis feels okay for daily use and the halves can be separated by up to 30 cm, which is great for comfort. There are rubber feet to keep each half steady. The fixed USB Type-A cable feels very dated in 2025. The shine-through ABS keycaps are fine to type on, but the legends are noticeably all over the place. That paired with the build quality makes them feel cheap. On the positive side, there are two columns of macro keys that will appeal to many MMORPG players.

Kinesis’s

SmartSet

app is straightforward, with no installation or sign-in needed. You can remap keys, record macros, and adjust per-key RGB lighting with static or reactive effects. It lacks VIA or QMK support, but at least there is onboard memory that keeps your settings intact across systems. The lighting looks good, but not particularly noteworthy.

Latency averaged 13.1 ms, which is a little slower than the average for a wired connection but it's not anything dealbreaking by any margin. Though there have been comparable keyboards that offer better performance that would be easier to recommend. The Cherry MX Blue switches sound and feel exactly how you’d expect: loud, sharp, and tactile, with a bit of hollowness from the case. At least it is a hot-swapple keyboard so you can replace it with a set of linear switches.

Interestingly, the split layout might also appeal to gamers who want extra mouse room. Using just the left half frees up plenty of space without losing access to essential keys which can be handy for low-sensitivity players or cramped setups.

name: Corsair CX550
text:
A serviceable $70 ATX 2.31 power supply that covers the basics but falters when pushed beyond everyday use.

The CX550 comes in very basic packaging with a cardboard box, a thin piece of foam, and only the essentials inside. It measures 140 × 86 × 125 mm, though the fixed cables make the effective depth closer to 150 mm. Corsair lists a weight of 2.28 kg, but our sample measured 1.798 kg(including all of the permanently attached cables), which is quite a notable difference.

Cables are permanently attached. The 24-pin, CPU, and PCIe lines are sleeved, while SATA and Molex are bare. Flexibility is average, and the non-modular layout makes cable routing more difficult. Cooling is provided by a single 120 mm fan that does not feature a hybrid/ECO mode.

This is a straightforward ATX 2.31 power supply with no hybrid fan modes or software controls. The 5-year warranty is below the current industry average of 7 to 10 years, but not unexpected at this price.

Corsair does not list any protection features for this model, and in our testing it failed the standard protection tests we perform, completely failing to function after OPP testing.  The protections fields have been populated according to the information on

this Corsair page

. While the protections failure is not ideal, it is unlikely to affect users running modest systems that will not reach the power supply’s limits.

Load regulation stayed within ATX specifications, though the 12 V rail dropped slightly under full load and the minor rails showed far more variation. Ripple performance was acceptable, with the 12 V rail peaking in the mid-70 mV range and the others staying well below limits.

This power supply specifies compliance to ATX 2.31 so it is not required to pass excursion testing, but we have conducted it out of curiosity.  The power supply was able to sustain the reduced excursion loads of up to 150% for the shorter 0.1 ms and 1 ms durations, but failed for the longer ones.

The conclusion from these tests is that this unit should not be used for performance hardware where stability is critical.  It has no problem complying with the limits set by the ATX specification and should be sufficient for a stable system.

name: Epomaker F75 (Epomaker x Aula)
text:
A budget-friendly 75% keyboard that punches above its weight with tri-mode connectivity, solid acoustics, and enthusiast-grade feel.

The keyboard comes with everything you need and some spare switches and a keycap/keyswitch puller (nice). The construction of the board is all plastic but surprisingly does not feel “cheap”. That can be attributed to the 4 layers of padding paired with the gasket mounting. Additionally, the bottom of the case is completely covered with a molded “silicon pad” that helps with the heft and the acoustics. Unlike some of the other all-plastic boards we’ve come across that are held together by clips, this keyboard was relatively easy to take apart for cleaning, maintenance, or upgrades.

The inclusion of the knob with an aluminum accent ring is a nice touch. This is a nitpick, but the legend alignment and font thickness on the Double-Shot PBT keycaps is slightly off in places. It’s not horrible, and somewhat admissible given the price point, but it may irk some users. What is good to have at this price point though, is the tri-mode connectivity with onboard storage for the receiver. The keyboard can be paired with up to 3 devices which is also very helpful.

The keyboard uses the

Epomaker x Aula F75 driver

software for all of its key-mapping, macro programming, and addressable per-key RGB backlighting. There is also configurable debounce time, however, only in n “stages” that don’t tell us the actual time durations. Caps lock has its own dedicated light on the bezel of the keyboard which is quite nice. You cannot remap the knob from the default volume controls using the software but we were able to use

Windows PowerToys

to remap it.

All that being said, the user interface and experience are serviceable but not great by any stretch of the imagination. Like the

Epomaker Magcore 87

that we recently reviewed, this “driver software” is only for this specific keyboard, which makes it quite annoying.

The keyboard tested quite well in our testing. The average latencies were 4.6 ms, 7.94 ms for wired and the 2.4GHz. Bluetooth trailed behind with 19 ms of latency, which is not bad at all for Bluetooth. The latencies are well below the average keyboard, especially in this price range.

Typing on the Graywood V3 switches was not the most consistent experience as some switches felt slightly grainy while others were smooth. For example, you can see using the actuation graphs that it took more force to actuate the “M” key than it did to actuate the “8” key. They sound more thocky than clacky which can be another big win for those looking for this sound profile without breaking the bank.

name: Razer Cobra Gengar Edition
text:
A $59.99 wired gaming mouse with solid 1.71 ms latency, consistent tracking, and a playful Gengar-themed design that adds flair (and a $20 markup) without changing any of the Cobra hardware.

It comes with just the documentation and a sticker. Build quality feels solid with no creaks or flex. The purple finish looks great, and the two Gengar prints on the main buttons just make you grin every time you look at them. The patterned palm shell feels quite good to hold. RGB lighting is modest for a Razer product and suits the theme nicely without going overboard. The only concern is how well the printed art on the buttons will hold up over time.

The Gengar Edition is functionally identical to the regular Cobra, just dressed up for the fans. It’s a wired-only mouse, so no wireless pairing headaches. Everything runs through

Razer Synapse

, which still feels like installing a small operating system just to change your DPI.

A reddit post

describes some testing that OP did which was quite interesting if not unsurprising. On the bright side, you do get full control over profiles, macros, and lighting once you log in. Lift-off distance, debounce time, and motion sync aren’t adjustable, but the essentials work fine once you fight your way through the bloat.

Latency averages 1.71 ms, which is right in line with most wired 1000 Hz gaming mice. It feels quick and consistent in use, with no noticeable stutters or hiccups. Tracking is very consistent, though it slightly overshoots the set DPI values. It’s not really a problem, but worth noting if you’re extra particular about accuracy. The sensor feels stable, and the measured lift-off distance of about 1.3 mm is a little on the high side and can’t be adjusted in Synapse.

name: Corsair HX1000i SHIFT
text:
A fully modular 1000 W ATX 3.1 PSU with side-mounted connectors and iCUE LINK integration, built for clean cable management.

The HX1000i SHIFT comes with quite a few items due to the iCUE LINK capabilities.  It is packaged with all of the standard accessories in addition to three cables for connecting the power supply to the motherboard and two other iCUE LINK devices.  

The included cables all have Corsair Type-5 connectors except for the 12V-2x6 cable which uses the standard connection.  They are very flexible and there should be no issues with cable management in any atypical SHIFT compatible cases.

The effective length of this case is unusual since the power output doesn't connect to the front.  For the measurement of the effective length we plugged in the USB Type-A cable and iCUE LINK cable.  Then we measured the 'effective width' of the power supply to be 180mm with the cables plugged in.

Corsair has recently been packaging power supplies entirely in cardboard and recyclable materials but this is an exception, packaged in foam blocks.  It could be that the higher weight of the power supply requires this method of packaging.  Our unit arrived without any damage or blemishes, but clearly during our testing and handing it collected some scratches.  We weren't particularly rough with this unit so it

may

be more prone to scratches.

The HX1000i SHIFT lacks the

Fan Control Knob

of some other recent models but it does support a Zero RPM Fan Mode which will prevent the fan from spinning until a 500W output load is reached.  In combination with the 140mm Fluid Dynamic Bearing fan, it is not surprising that it achieved a Cybenetics LAMBDA A+ certification.

The protections testing that we conducted found that the OPP and 12V OCP triggered around a very reasonable 124%.  The minor rails were slightly higher but have a lower maximum rating so are less likely to cause any hazards.

The voltage ripple/noise performance is unusual as it differs quite a bit depending on the input voltage used.  It shows quite good performance when on 115V input but the ripple increases quite significantly for 230V input.  This takes it from 'typical' noise to slightly high, though it is still within ATX specifications.

The load regulation performed as expected for a quality power supply, though it failed to maintain ATX voltage regulation specifications on the 12V rail for most of the excursions.  These tests simulate incredibly high power draws from the power supply(up to 200%) so this likely won't be an issue for typical use, but there are power supplies available that will sustain these tests and provide more stable power under extreme loads.

name: ASUS ROG Harpe II Ace Mouse Demon1 Edition
text:
A $169.99 lightweight, competition-ready mouse that keeps things simple, fast, and precise without overcomplicating its design.

The ROG Harpe II Ace Demon1 Edition we received came in a larger press kit with other variants, so the photos shown aren’t representative of retail packaging. The mouse itself feels excellent in hand, with a lightweight 48 g plastic shell and a matte coating that’s dry, grippy, and premium to the touch. Build quality is solid with no creaks or flex, and while it’s too early to tell if the finish will pick up grime over time, it currently resists fingerprints well. The PTFE feet glide smoothly, and the included spares and grip tape are practical touches that round out the package.

The mouse uses Asus’ new

Gear Link

software, which replaces Armoury Crate for peripheral control. It’s lightweight, easy to navigate, and doesn’t require account registration. There’s support for up to five onboard profiles and full macro programming, though cloud sync isn’t available yet. Gear Link works on Windows only, leaving macOS users without configuration support. It’s a welcome improvement over previous ROG software as it is faster, cleaner, and refreshingly minimal.

Asus equips the Harpe II Ace Demon1 Edition with its in-house ROG optical switches, which deliver crisp, consistent clicks without noticeable pre-travel. Latency performance is excellent, averaging 0.86 ms wired and 1.56 ms over the receiver, both comfortably within top-tier territory. Bluetooth mode is much slower at 12.44 ms but not terrible for Bluetooth.

The Aimpoint Pro Optical Sensor’s tracking feels stable and predictable, and the high ceiling on resolution and polling rate gives players more headroom than most will ever need. Overall, the Harpe II Ace feels tuned for precision and low latency, with performance that matches its competitive focus.

name: Keychron Black Myth Wukong Deluxe Edition
text:
A keyboard fit for a legend, this 75% board pairs smooth linear switches with a sturdy build and a charming gourd knob, though you might need Wukong’s treasure hoard to afford it at $259.99.

The Wukong Deluxe Edition arrives with a surprisingly extensive bundle: a USB Type-C cable, 2.4 GHz receiver with an extension adapter, extra keycaps, switches, feet, gasket mounts, tools, and even a decorative gourd knob and metal keycap. The packaging feels appropriately lavish for a collector’s edition, and for some, the themed accessories will add to its charm. Still, at nearly $300, it’s understandable that others (like our tester) might wish the extras leaned more toward functional customization rather than purely aesthetic flair.

The keyboard itself makes a strong case for the price once you start typing.
