The aluminum chassis and polycarbonate plate give it real heft and stability, while the internal gasket mount helps keep vibrations in check. It doesn’t creak, flex, or collect fingerprints, and the overall build feels premium. The gourd knob might look like it’s in the way, but in practice, it rarely interferes. If anything, it’s a fun reminder that this board isn’t afraid to lean into its mythology.

The Black Myth Wukong Deluxe Edition doesn’t try to reinvent the wheel here, but what’s included works well enough. It runs on

Keychron Launcher

, which handles RGB lighting, macros, and remapping for a single onboard profile. It’s a “set it and forget it” kind of software experience, which feels appropriate for a board that’s more about aesthetics than tinkering.

RGB lighting is handled cleanly, with per-key control and a range of presets that don’t overpower the board’s subdued gold-and-black theme. The lighting stays soft and it is bright enough for a dark room.

Typing on the Wukong Deluxe Edition feels satisfyingly smooth thanks to the TTC Golden Pink linear switches. They’re light, consistent, and produce a muted thock that fits right in with the board’s premium presentation. The switches are also hot-swappable, so if you ever want to give Wukong a different voice, that option’s there.

Latency performance is strong across all modes. Wired mode averaged around 2.6 ms, with the 2.4 GHz receiver measuring just below 5 ms. Bluetooth, as expected, lags behind at around 18 ms, but it should remain consistent and usable for casual work setups.

name: be quiet! Pure Power 12 M 650W
text:
An efficient mid-range power supply that delivers solid performance across the board, with standout excursion results and reliable protections for its class.

Comes with only the essentials: PSU, modular cables, AC cable, bolts, and zip-ties. Build quality feels solid and the compact chassis leaves enough room for most cases. Cable quality is decent, with sleeved 24-pin and 12VHPWR cables and slightly inflexible ribbons elsewhere. The 12VHPWR is limited to 450 W in lieu of the standard 600W. Although, this is not surprising for a 650W power supply. 

The 120 mm fan is always on, so the unit should run cool. Ratings and specifications match expectations for a 650 W Gold ATX 3.0 supply with no Cybenetics certification.

The feature set is straightforward. The PSU runs a single constant-fan mode and includes the full suite of standard protections plus Surge & Inrush Protection (SIP). 

SIP helps protect against sudden current spikes when you plug in the power supply or if voltage surges occur on the power line. All modern PSUs include some level of this protection.

You also get a 10-year manufacturer warranty which is good and on par with the competition in this price range and specifications.

Ripple performance is comfortably within ATX limits, with the 12V rail peaking around 36 mV under 30% of its rated load and stabilizing around 30 mV for 30 - 100% of the rated load. The minor rails peak near the 25 mV mark. These are average results for this wattage and price bracket and shouldn’t cause any stability issues. 

Load regulation is similarly steady, with the 12V rail dropping only about 0.140 V from idle to full load. All rails stayed well inside ATX specifications. 

Excursion testing was excellent – the unit sustained every 200% load transient without fault and kept voltages in spec.

name: Keychron M6 Lite
text:
A comfortable, feature-packed ergonomic mouse that blends productivity design with gaming-grade performance at $49.99 USD.

The Keychron M6 Lite feels solid in hand, with a comfortable ergonomic shape reminiscent of MX Master-style designs. The matte plastic shell has no flex and supports a full palm grip naturally. Both scroll wheels, a primary notched wheel and a smooth thumb wheel (typically horizontal scrolling), feel well-built and add real versatility for productivity or creative work. The clicks are sharp, the side buttons firm, and the PTFE feet glide cleanly across different surfaces. The included dual receivers and USB adapter are a rare bonus at this price.

Keychron Launcher

handles all customization for the M6 Lite, offering up to five onboard profiles with full macro programming and remapping support. It works on both Windows and macOS without requiring registration or cloud sync. The M6 Lite also includes dual scroll wheels: a standard top wheel and a thumb wheel that can be assigned to functions like horizontal scrolling or app-specific shortcuts, making it particularly useful for productivity tasks.

The M6 Lite’s Huano silent switches keep office environment users happy. There is no noticeable pre-travel or wobble. Latency performance is impressive for an ergonomic mouse, staying competitive with many gaming mice over both wired and 2.4 GHz modes, while Bluetooth naturally trails behind. The

PAW3311

sensor, though not the best in class, tracks somewhat reliably across surfaces, but it does tend to overshoot by a significant amount. With the 1000Hz polling rate it punches above its class in terms of performance for a really good price.

name: Keychron Q16 HE 8K
text:
A $229.99 ceramic keyboard that’s beautiful, innovative, and instantly covered in smudges.

The Q16 HE goes all in on its ceramic experiment. The box is stocked with the basics and spare feet, screws, keycaps, and a cleaning cloth – an item you’ll actually need since both the chassis and keycaps are fingerprint magnets.

The 65% layout keeps the footprint small on the desk. At 933 grams, it’s lighter than many aluminum builds (and what you would expect from a full ceramic keyboard) but still quite hefty. The tray mount uses soft silicone bushings to cushion the plate, which helps, though a gasket mount would have been a better match for the premium materials. Some fit and finish issues hold it back, like a stripped standoff and crossthreaded screw, but overall it feels sturdy, smooth, and unlike anything else to type on. Notably, we did get a pre-production sample so your mileage may vary.

Keychron Launcher

runs in the browser, so it works on any OS without downloads or logins. The interface is clean and responsive, making it easy to adjust actuation or lighting. Firmware updates were a bit unreliable during testing, but otherwise the software behaved well. Oddly, the board ships in 6-KRO mode; you’ll need to press Fn + N to unlock full N-KRO, which isn’t mentioned in the quick-start guide.

The Q16 HE supports adjustable actuation, rapid trigger, and multi-action mapping, allowing you to assign different functions based on press depth or speed. It’s an impressive feature set for gaming or workflow tweaks, but the wired-only connection unfortunately means that you will not get to enjoy that freedom untethered.

The Keychron Lime magnetic switches are smooth and consistent, though the sound profile leans bright and clacky instead of the deep tone you might expect from the ceramic body. Foam layers inside help control the resonance though.

Latency averaged 1.33 ms in wired mode, which is excellent for any keyboard, gaming or otherwise. The experience is fast and stable, with no stutters or missed inputs. Prelubed stabilizers work fine apart from a slightly sticky spacebar on our unit, which doesn’t significantly affect use.

name: Epomaker Magcore 87
text:
One of the first consumer keyboards to feature inductive switches, the $199 USD Magcore 87 pairs magnetic precision with premium build quality and clean acoustics.

While there is nothing unexpected in the box, the build quality paired with the feel of the aluminum are excellent. The aluminum adds quite a bit of heft to the normal TKL sized keyboard. I like the heft to it but it is a matter of preference so - to each their own. The keycap texture is smooth and feels nice to type on.

This is one of the first widely available keyboards with inductive switches which is really cool. It was cool enough for us to

CT scan it

because why not? Inductive keyboard switches use

electromagnetic induction

to detect key presses without physical contact, relying on a coil and a tiny metal rod that moves through the coil with the actuation of the key. This means that it comes with all feature sets that analogue keyboards often come with: adjustable actuation and rapid trigger (configurable in the driver).

The

Epomaker Magcore 87 Driver

is not bad but it is a little barebones. It is a “driver software”, meaning there is a different

one for each of their product lines

. It does beg the question why Epomaker can’t just make one app to rule them all. 

There were plenty of lighting options to choose from so many will find something they’ll like. Although, the tester did wish that the lighting of the Epomaker logo was not restricted to the rainbow cycle effect. Additionally, it can only store one onboard profile and no cloud sync which is a tiny oof at the price point. Hey, at least there is macro programming.

Typing on the Magcore 87 feels excellent. The Kailh Box Inductive switches are smooth, consistent, and linear, with a relatively short pre-travel out of the box (which is customizable of course) that makes each press feel quick without being hair-trigger sensitive. It feels closer to the familiar satisfying mechanical board than something overly engineered. Combined with the dense aluminum body, keystrokes land with a thock. Though the spacebar did feel slightly “hollow”.

Latency results were solid, coming in at around 7 ms. It’s not the fastest wired board we’ve ever tested, but it’s responsive enough that most players won’t notice a delay. The data from our analyzer tool looked a bit noisy for what’s supposed to be 8000 Hz polling rate but based on the other results, their claim is likely true. In practice, inputs felt instantaneous, so whatever chaos is happening in the signal seems purely technical and not something you’ll ever feel.

name: Phanteks Revolt 1600W Titanium
text:
The Phanteks Revolt 1600W is an ATX 3.0 power supply with an additional fan mode that takes the modular cable concept to a whole new level.

Despite not including any cables, this power supply comes with quite a bit in the box.  This includes all of the standard accessories, as well as a PSU tester which can be quite useful for testing functionality or using the power supply for purposes other than your computer.

The most notable 'feature' of this power supply is the lack of included cables, though they do include zip-ties and velcro cables ties, seemingly rubbing it in.

The chassis of this power supply is understandably quite large, packing in many components and bringing it to a total 2.945 kg.  The rear of the chassis features the expected power plug and switch, along with the fan mode switch.

Also note that the input voltage rating of this power supply is 115-240V, deviating from the typical 100-240V.  Raising the minimum input voltage is common for 1600W power supplies as lower voltages require additional current, generating more heat for the power supply to dissipate.

The power supply features all of the standard protections that we expect and they performed well in our testing.  The combined 125W limit of the 5V and 3.3V rails isn't low, but the 1600W capability of the 12V rail does make it stand out.

The collaboration with Seasonic on the design of this power supply shows with the low ripple noise of this power supply.  It remains under 30mV of ripple noise across all rails, despite delivering up to 1600W.  This isn't quite as low as measured with the

Seasonic PRIME TX-1600W(Noctua Edition)

, but should cause no issues.  The excursion performance was just as impressive.  It supplied power through all of the excursions, and the ATX regulation failures that did occur were minor.

There should be absolutely no issues using this model to supply multiple high power draw components.

name: Womier SK75 TMR
text:
A heavy, premium-feeling 75% TMR keyboard with smooth switches, strong latency numbers, and surprising flexibility at USD $129.99 is a solid buy.

The SK75 TMR ships with the usual assortment of a cable, receiver, and tools, but the inclusion of extra switches and keycaps is a welcome bonus at this price point. Its 2 kg weight makes it feel reassuringly solid on the desk or, in a pinch, useful as a blunt instrument. The aluminum chassis with FR4 plate feels quite premium, and the rear “chroma mirror” weight gives it some visual flair, but the plate-mounted stabilizers keep it from reaching truly high-end territory.

The stock PBT keycaps should be durable and are pleasant to use, though the lack of shine-through means the RGB is more of a side feature than a centerpiece. Connectivity is versatile with wired, Bluetooth, and 2.4 GHz options. However, there’s no onboard storage for the receiver, leaving it vulnerable to being lost to couch crevices. 
A standout here is that the SK75 isn’t locked to magnetic switches - the PCB also includes hotswap sockets for conventional mechanical switches, giving users more flexibility than many other magnetic boards.

The companion software does its job, offering the expected suite of RGB controls, key remapping, and macro programming, but the interface feels cluttered and not especially intuitive. It also nudges you toward making an account to sync profiles and share lighting setups, which is optional, but not exactly enticing.

Where it earns points is in the TMR feature set. You can tweak actuation points, enable rapid trigger, set up

SOCD

(Simultaneous Opposite Cardinal Directions) cleaning to get banned from Valorant (which is good for your mental health), and even play with dynamic keystroke behavior. These options give it a level of adjustability that many other boards in this price range can’t match. Still, the wireless mode switch being tucked under the caps lock key is an odd design choice that makes swapping between modes more of a chore than it needs to be.

Latency results, while they are not some of the best we’ve seen, are excellent across all modes: 1.8 ms wired, 2.1 ms over the 2.4 GHz receiver, and even Bluetooth stays at a respectable (for Bluetooth) 8.0 ms. That puts it well ahead of the “average” keyboard, where 10 ms is common for wireless.

The Outemu magnetic switches used here are linear and impressively smooth thanks to the contactless design. Test numbers show an operating force of about 42 gf with 1.92 mm of pre-travel and a total travel distance of 3.59 mm. This lands them in a sweet spot for both gaming and typing (at least for the author), with a light key start force (31 gf) that makes the board feel snappy without being too twitchy.

Sound-wise, the board comes across denser than most at this price because of the stacked foams and aluminum case, but the infamous spacebar ping that haunts most keyboards in this price range is still prevalent.

name: Logitech MX Master 4
text:
The classic ‘if it ain’t broke, don’t fix it’ Logitech mouse gets a subtle refresh, adding a few smart features that could win over a new wave of productivity enthusiasts.

The MX Master 4 keeps things simple in the box with just the mouse, a USB-C receiver, and some paperwork. At this price, it feels a bit stingy not to throw in a cable, especially since you’ll eventually need to charge it.

Once out of the box, though, the mouse makes a strong first impression. The finish feels more refined than previous MX generations, and the rubberized haptic panel is quite grippy. 

The 150 g (9g heavier than the last generation) weight gives it a solid, planted feel but the additional weight from the previous generation could bother some.

The customizable haptics are the real draw. Being able to tie tactile feedback to shortcuts or actions feels fresh and the adjustable scroll “notchy-ness” is genuinely satisfying.

Logi Options+

runs the show, but forcing a login to grab haptic plugins is annoying. The marketplace-style browser for shortcut packs is clever, just less so when it’s locked behind an account. While the "Action Rings" do handle inputs for supported apps we wouldn't call it macro programming and with only one onboard profile, flexibility is limited, but you do get cloud sync.

The MX Master 4’s clicks are dramatically quieter than past models, trading crisp tactility for soft, muted presses that suit offices or late-night use.

Latency is inconsistent over Bluetooth LE, averaging 11.7 ms with occasional stutters. While the included receiver's encrypted Bluetooth connection (

what Logitech refers to as Bolt

) might help with reliable pairing, we did not measure it having an impact on latency. This is not a gaming mouse, and the wireless performance makes that clear.

Tracking is serviceable for productivity, with an 8,000 DPI sensor and a 125 Hz polling rate (oof) that can feel a bit wonky at times. The MX Master 4, with its quite low polling rate, was clearly not designed for high acceleration rates so it stops tracking well when moving at high speeds (as seen in our testing at 5000mm/s). The MagSpeed scroll wheel still offers fast free-spin and precise ratchet modes, though it rattles side-to-side like the last model and smooth mode feels nearly identical with slightly less inertia.

name: Corsair Vanguard Pro 96
text:
A compact 96% board with magnetic sorcery, Stream Deck tricks, and esports-ready speed for $229.99 USD.

It comes with the basics plus a wristrest, a small branded Corsair keycap puller. The 96% layout feels standard in footprint, and at just over a kilo it sits steady on the desk.

The build quality is solid but the ABS housing makes it feel less premium than expected, with only the top plate in aluminum. The wrist rest is smoother and firmer than the K100’s quilted one but might be more prone to sweat and dirt. The keycaps are double-shot PBT, decent but unremarkable. 

The connectivity is wired-only over a seemingly awkwardly placed USB Type-C port with a cable pass-through on the underside that lets you route the cable neatly in whatever direction you would desire.

The Vanguard Pro 96 uses

Corsair Web Hub

, a browser-based utility that handles per-key RGB, actuation tuning, and macros without needing a heavy software install. You get five onboard profiles and onboard memory, though it’s not open-source like

QMK

or

VIA

.

Lighting is what we have come to expect from Corsair: per-key RGB with the usual presets. The LCD screen adds some flair, letting you upload images or system stats alongside said RGB effects.

Unique to this board are six low-profile G-keys on the side. They’re well-placed to avoid accidental presses, and with Elgato Stream Deck integration they’re more useful than the typical “macro row,” doubling for shortcuts in games, productivity apps, or streaming setups.

There’s also a “Gamer Mode” button that toggles a high-performance profile, though it feels a bit gimmicky given you can just lock 8,000 Hz polling in software.

Latency is excellent, averaging just 0.8 ms over wired, which puts it firmly in the top tier of gaming keyboards. Combined with Rapid Trigger and adjustable actuation down to 0.1 mm, it’s more than competitive for esports-level play.

Corsair’s MGX Hyperdrive magnetic switches feel smooth and consistent, with a light 40 g actuation. They’re linear and hotswappable, so you can swap in other compatible magnetic switches if you want a different feel. The typing sound is quieter and more refined than Corsair’s older K100, though purists might still find it a bit plain.

name: MSI MAG A750BN PCIE5
text:
The MSI MAG A750BN is a permanently connected power supply with minimal accessories and acceptable performance in our testing.

This power supply is packaged with truly the bare minimum of accessories.  It only has the cable to connect to the wall, and mounting bolts to connect to the case.  It doesn't even come with additional cables because they're all permanently attached.  It is difficult to expect much more when considering the low MSRP.  Our testing did confirm the claimed 80PLUS Bronze rating of the power supply.

The MAG A750BN is advertised with all of the standard protections except for UVP.  This feature is designed to protect your computer from dropping below ATX specifications and causing

undefined behaviour

.  In our brownout/dropout testing, the MAG A750BN showed that it was very responsive to low output voltages and would adequately signal to the computer that the voltages were unstable.(PG low signal)

While all of our voltage ripple/noise measurements remain within the ATX specification, they're not particularly impressive.  The 12V rail, the most important for low noise operation, ramps up quite steeply as the output wattage is increased.  This may present stability issues if you are using this power supply in a system with high power components and operating near the upper limit.

Load regulation performance is solid across the rails and this is mirrored with the excursion results.  This is quite the feat for the power supply as it is not advertised to be compliant with ATX 3.0(or 3.1), and is not expected to meet the same requirements.  We conducted the excursion testing anyway and the MAG A750BN performed quite well, only failing to sustain a couple 100ms excursions and dipping below the ATX voltage specification for a couple other test runs.

name: Corsair Sabre v2 Pro Ultralight Wireless
text:
A featherweight full-shell mouse proving that ultralight numbers can be hit without cutting holes in the shell (or your wallet).

Inside the box you get the mouse, a USB Type-A to Type-C cable, a USB Type-C wireless receiver, replacement PTFE feet, grip tape, and an alcohol pad so you can clean the shell before applying it (nice).

The Sabre V2 Pro Ultralight feels solid in hand despite its weight. Corsair avoided the honeycomb cutouts used by many ultralights and still managed to hit a similar weight target with a full plastic shell.

Connectivity is limited to wired and 2.4 GHz wireless. There is no Bluetooth or multipoint pairing, so it is clearly focused on gaming rather than multi-device productivity. The detachable 180 cm USB Type-C cable works for charging or wired play, and the receiver is plug-and-play without any noticeable quirks.

During testing we had to use the beta version of the

Corsair Web Hub

, but it has officially released as of the time of writing. The web interface makes it quick to adjust settings without a bulky install, which I personally prefer.  You do get macro programming and four onboard profiles that save directly to the mouse, though there’s no cloud sync.

Latency testing results are average, with 8.83 ms over wired and 9.02 ms on the 2.4 GHz receiver. That puts it in line with most mainstream wireless mice, not a top-tier outlier but also free of stutters or spikes so it is quite average.

The Marksman S sensor tracked well throughout testing. It supports up to 33,000 DPI in 50-step increments and a maximum polling rate of 8000 Hz. In practice, DPI tracking felt accurate and responsive without any odd jumps or skips.

Click feel is on the quieter side, with Corsair’s mechanical switches providing a muted response that still feels satisfying.

name: Super Flower Combat FG 650W ATX 3.1
text:
The Combat FG 650W ATX 3.1 is a fully modular power supply with some impressive test results, as long as you can excuse the failures we experienced with protections testing.

The Combat FG 650W includes all the expected accessories along with a molex to FDD adapter, which may come in handy for legacy builds or odd use cases.  If not now, then it can be stowed in the included cable bag until a special occasion arises.  Except for the braided sleeve 24-pin motherboard cable, the cables are of the flat ribbon style and can be a little stiff when working with them.  

It is also worth noting that the included 12V-2x6 cable is only rated for 300W, not the maximum - and more common - 600W.

The power supply holds Gold level 80PLUS and Cybenetics ETA efficiency ratings as well as a Standard++ Cybenetics Lambda rating.  This slightly louder rating from Cybenetics is not a surprise as the power supply only has a 120mm fan, though the open grill design allows for good airflow.

The Combat FG 650W only has a single fan mode and it does not stop spinning at low loads.  Given the slightly louder sound profile, a hybrid mode would be a welcome addition.

The unit claims to offer all the standard protections except Under Voltage Protection (UVP). However, given that both of our samples failed in protections testing, those claims deserve some scrutiny.  The tested samples were successful in preventing a serious hazard, but they damaged themselves, and have the potential to damage equipment connected to them.

The voltage ripple/noise performance of this power supply is underwhelming and bordering on poor.  For basic systems, this is likely not a major issue, but it is not suitable for performance focused builds.

Load regulation was solid. The output rails only fluctuated by 0.116V across the full load range. That makes the inconsistent excursion performance even more surprising. As ambient temperature increased, the PSU began to fail some of the excursion tests and produced voltage spikes that exceeded ATX specifications on the 12V rail.

As this is a low wattage power supply it is unlikely that you'll be connecting high end hardware, but you may eventually upgrade other components of your computer and a power supply upgrade should be considered alongside that.

name: HyperX Pulsefire SAGA pro wireless
text:
A feature-packed wireless gaming mouse with solid customization but the latency performance is a little underwhelming at this price point

Inside the box, HyperX includes quite a few extras alongside the Pulsefire Saga Pro Wireless. You get the braided USB Type-A to USB Type-C cable, a USB Type-A wireless receiver with an adapter, grip tape, and extra PTFE feet. They also throw in two additional button covers, two interchangeable side button pairs, and even two different shells, making it possible to customize the mouse to your preference.

The mouse itself is built from plastic with a matte coating, giving it a lightweight yet sturdy feel in hand. The PTFE feet provide smooth glide, and with the included spare set, long-term durability shouldn’t be an issue. At 180 cm, the detachable braided cable is long enough for most setups. Overall, the construction feels solid for a wireless gaming mouse, and the swappable shells and buttons add an extra layer of flexibility that sets it apart from more basic competitors.

The Pulsefire Saga Pro Wireless relies on HyperX’s

NGENUITY

software for customization. You only get a single profile, so it doesn't give you the ability to have a gaming vs. productivity/other mode that you can quickly switch between. There’s no cloud sync either, so everything stays local, leaving it feeling barebones compared to more fully featured suites from competitors. Another downside is that NGENUITY still only runs on Windows, cutting off macOS users entirely.

Macro programming is supported, so you can bind functions to the extra side buttons. HyperX includes swappable shells and buttons in the box, and even goes a step further by publishing official

3D-printing files

for users who want to create their own custom parts. This gives the Saga Pro a level of physical customization that most mice can’t match.

Note: The software is in its beta phase as of the time of writing this so it may not have all of the features now and things may be changing/updating in the future.

The Pulsefire Saga Pro Wireless turns in acceptable but not outstanding latency numbers. Wired mode averages 2.42 ms and the 2.4 GHz receiver comes in at 3.09 ms. Both are good enough for most players, but they fall short of the sub-2 ms figures you can find on other mice, especially in the same price range. Bluetooth, meanwhile, lands way out of competitive territory at 14.38 ms, making it usable only for general desktop work. The HyperX 26K sensor offers a wide DPI range from 200 to 26000 in 50-step increments, and the 4000Hz maximum polling rate allows tracking to stay consistent throughout. Lift-off distance is under 1 mm, which is excellent for gamers who need frequent resets during fast movements.

name: Keychron M5 Wireless Ergonomic Mouse
text:
A vertical mouse that blends ergonomic comfort with gaming-grade performance at a competitive price.

Inside the box you’ll find the mouse itself, a USB Type-C to Type-C braided cable, a USB Type-A wireless receiver, two USB adapters, and the usual user documentation. Nothing flashy or unexpected here, but it covers all the essentials you need to get started.

The M5’s build quality is quite solid. The ABS chassis with a satin coating feels sturdy and high-quality, with no creaks or rattles when handled. Although, the satin coating might feel a little slippery for some grip styles. At 97 g, it’s fairly light for a vertical mouse, which some will enjoy for its ease of movement, while others may prefer a heavier design that feels more planted on the desk. The PTFE feet glide smoothly but there are no extras included in the box. The braided cable is serviceable though a bit stiff, which isn’t much of an issue for general use but isn’t ideal if you intend to use the mouse wired regularly.

The Keychron M5 is managed through the

Keychron Launcher

, a web app that’s simple to use and doesn’t require registration. You get up to five onboard profiles, and every button except the left click can be remapped or assigned macros, with onboard memory ensuring your settings carry across systems. There’s no cloud sync, so all configuration stays local.

For customization, the software goes beyond the basics. It allows independent debounce settings for the primary buttons, side buttons, and scroll wheel press. There are five DPI presets and six polling rate presets, controllable directly from buttons on the underside of the mouse and these changes sync automatically with your profiles.

Advanced options include angle snapping, ripple control (mouse smoothing), motion sync (improves tracking at the cost of latency), and a 20K FPS mode that increases the sensor framerate for tighter tracking (but the maximum polling rate is still 8KHz) at the expense of battery life. It is kind of like when your monitor has a refresh rate of 60Hz but it feels smoother if you have higher than 60 FPS in your games. Lift-off distance can be tuned between 0.7 mm, 1.0 mm (default), and 2.0 mm. Both the scroll wheels can also be reversed, a nice bonus for productivity use.

The M5 uses Huano mechanical switches that provide sharp, consistent clicks across the buttons. They feel fine for everyday use, though not as crisp or premium as the best implementations, and they aren’t hot-swappable if you ever wanted to change them.

Latency results are excellent for a vertical mouse. Wired mode averages 0.751 ms and the 2.4 GHz receiver sits at 0.929 ms, both well under 1 ms and effectively indistinguishable in use. Bluetooth comes in at 8.24 ms which is a little slower, but still quite good for Bluetooth.

Tracking is equally strong thanks to the PixArt PAW3950 sensor. Accuracy was very tight at 800, 1600, and 2400 DPI, with low-speed movement (200 mm/s) landing almost exactly on target and high-speed movement (5000 mm/s) showing only a small dip in precision. With a range of 50–30000 DPI in 1 step increments (insane), an 8000 Hz polling rate, and a customizable lift-off distance below 0.7 mm, the M5 easily meets gaming-level standards despite being positioned as an ergonomic mouse.

name: Redmagic GK001J Mechanical Keyboard
text:
A flashy 1800-style board with a knob and screen, but its high latency makes it a poor choice for gaming.

Out of the box you get the keyboard, a coiled USB Type-A to USB Type-C cable, a USB Type-A wireless receiver, documentation, and a keycap/switch puller. The coiled cable will appeal to some, but overall it’s just the standard stuff.

Construction is mostly plastic, but the gasket mounting and aluminum accent plates give it a more premium feel than expected. The torx-fastened brushed metal plate at the front ties into the futuristic aesthetic, while the knurled knob and small screen on the top right add a unique flair. At 1147 g, it feels solid enough not to slide around thanks to the L-shaped rubber feet. The dongle also tucks neatly into a slot underneath, which is a nice quality-of-life touch. The PBT keycaps have a fine texture and side-translucent legends that glow nicely with the RGB. They look aggressive and stylized (probably a love-it-or-hate-it situation) but feel good under the fingers.

Connectivity is tri-mode: wired USB Type-C, 2.4 GHz wireless, and Bluetooth 5.1 with up to three device pairings. Switching is handled through Fn (function) shortcuts, and the central USB Type-C port is accompanied by a mode toggle. Flexibility here is strong, though as we’ll see later, performance doesn’t live up to the promise.

The knob is detented and satisfying to turn, letting you adjust volume or interact with the small screen. While fun, the screen doesn’t offer much practical value beyond displaying a tiny picture,  showing system info or RGB modes. 

The

REDMAGIC Cloud Driver

software covers the basics: RGB effects, simple key remaps, and single-combo macros. You can’t record full sequences, and per-key lighting isn’t supported. It's more “set and forget” than deep customization. On the plus side, it works without mandatory logins or cloud accounts, but it’s nowhere near as capable as enthusiast firmware like QMK or VIA.

Here’s the deal breaker: latency. Despite advertising itself as a gaming keyboard, the GK001J is among the slowest we’ve tested. Wired mode averages just below 60 ms, Bluetooth lags further just above 60 ms and 2.4 GHz is at 50 ms. With a 1000 Hz polling rate, this shouldn’t be happening, so it likely comes down to a low scan rate or bandwidth being shared with the onboard display. Either way, the result is poor responsiveness that makes the board unsuitable for serious gaming.

The TTC Speed Silver V2 switches themselves are smooth and light, with a 40 g actuation and short 1.33 mm pre-travel. They deliver a fast feel in isolation, but paired with the latency issue, their advantage is wasted. Sound is on the clackier side, though the gasket mount helps tame hollowness, so overall acoustics are better than expected for a mostly plastic case.

name: Vetroo Vetroo 850W
text:
The Vetroo 850W is an ATX 3.1 power supply available in a black, white, or pink colour schemes at a reasonable price.

The Vetroo 850W has fairly standard construction quality but is lighter than other ATX form factor 850W power supplies we've tested.  This can be beneficial for portability but is sometimes a sign of fewer/lower quality components, this would correlate well with the failure we experienced.

The power supply is available in black, white, and pink colourways with a $4 USD premium for the pink model.  Pink power supplies are not common so this is a nice option to have, however, these are not the most complete colour schemes.  The connectors and fan grilles of the power supplies are black across all of the colourways, it also looks like the pink model uses white cables.

Included In the Box are the cables, an AC cable, documentation, mounting bolts, a cable bag, a PSU tester, and many velcro cable ties which allow it to be packaged nicely and will be useful later for cable management.  The cable ties were colour matched to our white model and will help fit the theme.

The power supply is equipped with a switch on the rear of the chassis to select between the normal and eco fan modes.  In eco mode, the fan will automatically shut down during "low to moderate operation modes".

The Vetroo 850W advertises all of the standard protections listed in this section; there were no hazards created from our testing so they've served at least part of their purpose.  However, we also expect that power supplies recover from protections being engaged, which this power supply failed to do in our testing.  Depending on your use-case for this power supply, the features and performance that it exhibits in other areas in addition to the the ten year warranty may make up for the protections failure.

The load regulation(voltage variance under load) of the 12V rail looks quite good under a static load like our load regulation test.  However, during excursion testing the voltage exceeded the maximum and minimum specification of the ATX standard.  The results for the 100ms excursions were compliant, but all of the other excursions caused it to exceed the specifications.  The output voltage deviated over 1V in either direction of the nominal 12V; we measured a minimum of 10.919V and a maximum of 13.022V, the highest we've seen yet.

With the exception of the 5Vsb rail, the voltage ripple of this power supply is very typical.  The 5Vsb isn't used in critical operation of any components so it shouldn't affect use, but the poor excursion results already disqualify this power supply for use with high performance components.

name: Keychron Q3
text:
A premium tenkeyless aluminum keyboard with hot-swap sockets, full QMK/VIA support, and a focus on sound and feel over wireless convenience.

The keyboard comes with everything you need to get started and a few tools for future tinkering. Inside the box, you’ll find the keyboard itself, a USB Type-C to Type-C cable, a USB Type-C to Type-A cable, a switch puller, a keycap puller, a screwdriver, and a hex key.

Measuring 4.2 cm tall, 36.4 cm wide, and 13.6 cm deep, the Q3 has a footprint typical of a tenkeyless layout but carries a serious weight at 1,988 g — heavy enough to stay planted during use without relying on grippy feet. The chassis is aluminum, paired with a steel base plate finished in black. Stabilizers are screw-in, which generally makes for a more secure fit and less rattle on larger keys.

The included keycaps are made from PBT plastic, use double-shot legends for long-term durability, and do not allow shine-through for RGB lighting.
Connectivity is wired-only through a detachable USB Type-C cable, with the computer and keyboard side connectors both using Type-C. For $50 more you can get the

Keychron Q3 Pro

, which has wireless connectivity. A physical switch on the rear lets you toggle between Windows and macOS layouts. The included cable measures 172.5 cm, giving you enough reach for most setups.

The Q3 works with

Keychron Launcher

for programming and lighting control. Registration isn’t required, and while it only supports a single onboard profile, that profile can store your custom mappings thanks to built-in memory. There’s no cloud sync, but macro programming is supported. The board is both QMK and VIA compatible, opening up extensive customization possibilities for remapping keys, creating complex shortcuts, or assigning multimedia functions. It’s fully functional with both Windows and macOS.

Backlighting is RGB with per-key illumination, adjustable through the Keychron Launcher. Since the keycaps are non-shine-through PBT, the lighting appears more as an underglow around the keys rather than through the legends.

Wired latency averaged 9.5 ms in testing, which is slightly better than the typical 10 ms average for wired keyboards. While not marketed as an esports keyboard, it feels responsive and consistent enough for everyday work and casual gaming without noticeable delay or stutter.

Our unit came fitted with Gateron G Pro Brown switches in a 3-pin configuration. These are tactile switches(as denoted by the "Brown" name), with an operating force of 39.75 gf, bottoming out at 49.74 gf, and a pre-travel of 2.83 mm. Total travel is 4.13 mm, and the north-facing orientation improves keycap compatibility. Key feel is light and smooth, and the hot-swappable sockets make it easy to swap in different switch types without soldering.

While the Q3’s gasket-mounted design does add some dampening, there’s still a hint of ping and stabilizer rattle under harder presses which is something modders may want to address for an even cleaner sound profile.

name: Sony Inzone KBD-H75
text:
Sony’s INZONE KBD-H75 is a high-performance gaming keyboard with Hall Effect switches, ultra-low latency, and deep software customization.

Opening the box, you get the essentials: the keyboard itself, a detachable USB Type-C to Type-A cable, a protective cloth keyboard cover, product documentation, and a set of Inzone stickers for a touch of branding flair. Nothing flashy, but it covers the basics.

Build quality is solid with minimal flex thanks to the aluminum top and aluminum plate. The bottom of the enclosure is plastic and feels a little cheap compared to the rest of the construction, but still acceptable. Stabilizers are plate-mounted, which should help keep larger keys reasonably steady.

At 3.5 cm tall and weighing 804 grams, the KBD-H75 is slim yet sturdy enough not to shift around on a desk. The footprint is 31.7 cm wide and 13.1 cm deep, which keeps it compact without feeling cramped.

The keycaps are PBT with doubleshot legends, so they are durable and resistant to shine over time. The RGB shine-through double-shot legends should keep their sharp edges instead of fading like cheaper pad-printed caps.

Connectivity is straightforward. It is a wired-only keyboard with a detachable USB Type-C to Type-A cable that measures 170 cm. There is no wireless mode, Bluetooth, or USB passthrough, which makes the feature set feel barebones but ensures a consistent, no-fuss connection. Clearly, everything was designed with the

gamer

gamers in mind.

The KBD-H75 is managed through Sony’s INZONE Hub software, which gives access to most of its customization features. You can store up to four profiles directly on the keyboard thanks to onboard memory, though there’s no cloud sync option for transferring  settings between devices. Macro programming is supported, but enthusiasts may be disappointed that it doesn’t support QMK or VIA configurators.

Compatibility is focused on Windows, which works seamlessly, but macOS support is missing. That means Mac users are out of luck unless they’re willing to live with default layouts and limited customization.

Backlighting is handled through the same INZONE Hub software and supports full RGB with per-key illumination. The shine-through PBT keycaps pair nicely with this, giving lighting effects a crisp, professional look without sacrificing durability.

One standout feature is the ability to fine-tune actuation points on the Hall Effect switches. This means you can set keys to trigger higher or lower depending on your preference. Paired with the Ultra Low Latency (ULL) mode, this can make the keyboard feel even faster and more responsive than its already excellent baseline performance.

Sony highlights the keyboard’s integration into the INZONE ecosystem as a key selling point. The idea is to unify your setup with their headsets, monitors, and other gear under a single software suite. It’s not as open as enthusiast solutions like QMK or VIA, but it does keep things streamlined for users who prefer plug-and-play simplicity.

Performance is where the KBD-H75 really shines. This might be one of the best keyboards we’ve tested to date.

Latency over a wired connection with the Ultra Low Latency (ULL) mode on is an incredible 0.7 ms, which puts it among the fastest keyboards available and essentially eliminates any concern about input lag. Since it doesn’t support wireless or Bluetooth, you’re locked into wired use, but that tradeoff clearly pays off in raw responsiveness.

The switches are Gateron Hall Effect linears, with hot-swap support for 3-pin Cherry MX-style stems. Their actuation force is measured at 41.83 gf with a bottom-out force of 52.69 gf, striking a sweet balance between effortless keystrokes and satisfying feedback. The pre-travel is 1.52 mm with a total travel distance of 3.61 mm, which makes them slightly snappier than traditional linears.

Key start force comes in at 34.87 gf, which the tester found to be just the right amount of force to consistently actuate the keys without accidental presses being a major issue. Combined with the consistency of Hall Effect sensing, keystrokes feel clean and precise. What takes it further is the combination of customizable actuation points with Sony’s ULL mode, which makes this board not only responsive out of the box but tunable for even faster reaction times depending on your playstyle.

The typing experience is smooth and quiet with a solid linear response. Stabilizers keep the larger keys steady, so rattling is minimal. Sound-wise, the board delivers a clean, muted tone with a touch of clack, which should appeal to gamers and typists alike.

name: Sony Inzone Mouse-A
text:
Sony’s INZONE Mouse-A is a featherweight competitive mouse that prioritizes speed and simplicity.

In the box, Sony includes the mouse itself, a USB Type-C to USB Type-A cable, a USB Type-C wireless receiver, and user documentation. They also add some thoughtful extras like a spare set of PTFE feet and INZONE stickers.

The build is all plastic, but it feels smooth in hand. While it gives a sleek appearance, we found that the surface picks up fingerprints and smudges fairly quickly, which can make it look smudgy after regular use. The shell still feels solid, with no flex or creaking, and the mouse keeps a lightweight profile that contributes to its overall comfort. The PTFE feet offer a smooth glide on most surfaces, and the extra set in the box is a welcome addition for long-term durability.

The INZONE Mouse-A is supported by Sony’s INZONE Hub software. Users can save up to four profiles to the onboard memory, so custom settings travel with the mouse even without the software running. However, there’s no cloud sync to pull profiles across multiple devices.

Customization options are on the lighter side. There’s no macro programming support, which may disappoint users who rely on complex key bindings for productivity or MMO gaming. On the plus side, INZONE Hub is available for Windows and there is a web version of the app but no support for macOS. That’s an interesting addition because it would let users tweak settings without downloading anything to their system, making the experience more flexible than many competitors.

Latency performance is solid, though not quite chart-topping. Over a wired connection, the INZONE Mouse-A averaged 1.8 ms, while the 2.4 GHz wireless receiver improved things slightly with an average of 1.17 ms. There is no Bluetooth mode available, so you’re limited to wired or receiver use, but both offer consistent responsiveness suitable for gaming.

Tracking is handled by the PAW3950 sensor with a resolution range from 100 to 30000 DPI, adjustable in steps of 50. Accuracy testing at 800 DPI showed a measured average of around 812 DPI, giving a precision error of 0.54% and accuracy within +3.96%. That’s a strong showing, with very little deviation from the target.

The sensor also supports a maximum polling rate of 8000 Hz, which is quickly becoming the norm, and is well above the current standard 1000 Hz mice and helps reduce latency further in high-refresh gaming setups. Lift-off distance is under 0.7 mm, keeping cursor drift minimal when repositioning the mouse.

For click tactility, Sony uses optical switches. These provide crisp, consistent clicks without the risk of double-click issues found in mechanical switches, though they do feel a little different compared to traditional mechanical switches. Unlike some enthusiast mice, the switches aren’t hot swappable, so you’re locked into the stock click feel.

name: Lofree Block
text:
A retro-styled wireless mechanical keyboard with knobs, POM switches, and an underwhelming typing experience that doesn’t quite match its price tag.

Out of the box, the Lofree Block includes the essentials: the keyboard itself, a braided USB Type-C to USB Type-A cable, a 2.4 GHz wireless dongle, and a quick start guide. There’s no keycap or switch puller, which feels like a strange omission considering the keyboard is hot-swappable. The keyboard’s construction leans entirely into its retro identity, with a glossy cream-colored plastic case and squared-off edges. It looks charming on the desk, but in the hands, the all-plastic build feels hollow and less premium than the price might suggest. Internally, it uses a gasket mount with foam layers between the plate and bottom case, aiming to soften keystrokes and reduce harshness, though that effect is somewhat diminished by the light frame.

The keycaps are custom double-shot PBT with a flat, squared profile that complements the aesthetic but may take some getting used to for those expecting sculpted or cylindrical tops. Their matte texture feels nice, and the legends are crisp and easy to read. As for connectivity, the Block supports Bluetooth 5.0 (with pairing for up to three devices), 2.4 GHz wireless through the included dongle, and standard wired use via USB Type-C. A dedicated orange knob on the top right corner toggles between connection modes, and the small OLED screen beside it displays the active input and battery level.

The keyboard doesn’t support any companion software, so there’s no way to remap keys, set macros, or update firmware. All customization happens on the board itself, and it’s limited. Lighting is restricted to white-only backlighting with 14 preset effects, adjustable via shortcuts for brightness and animation speed.

What it does have are two standout knobs: one for volume (with press-to-mute) and the other for switching between USB Type-C, Bluetooth, and 2.4 GHz wireless. Both feel tactile and solid. A small OLED screen displays battery and connection mode, which is handy, though not particularly deep in function. It’s a minimalist feature set that works, but you’ll quickly feel the limits if you’re used to deeper customization.

The Block isn’t breaking speed records, but it delivers usable latency across all three connection modes. Wired mode clocks in at 11.4 ms, 2.4 GHz wireless averages 15.4 ms, and Bluetooth trails behind at 42.2 ms. These numbers are fine for everyday use, but gamers and fast typists will want to reconsider, or stick to wired and receiver modes.

The keyboard uses TTC Block switches which are linear and factory-lubed, built around a Cherry MX-compatible stem. They actuate at around 39.15 g with a light start force of 34.55 g and bottom out at 49.36 g. Pre-travel is 1.53 mm, and total travel is 4.02 mm, which puts them squarely in the range of traditional linear switches. The force curve confirms a smooth, flat response with no tactile bump, making them fast but somewhat lacking in character. They sound pleasantly muted and refined, especially for a board this size, with a clean, low-pitched bottom-out. Unfortunately, the sound is let down by the case itself, which feels hollow and plasticky under the fingers.

name: FSP Group MEGA TI 1650W
text:
The FSP Group MEGA TI 1650W is a monstrous ATX 3.1 power supply that pushes the boundaries of what your wall socket can handle.

In the very large box is the power supply along with a wide variety of accessories, all packaged nicely with cardboard supports.  Particularly notable accessories are the PSU tester and cable bag which will come in handy to store all remaining cables after installation.  The large collection of provided cables will allow a lot of flexibility with connections and future expandability.  The cables also have a braided pattern to the insulation and are very flexible.

The output wattage of this power supply is 1650W, with a heavy asterisk, it requires an input voltage of 200-240Vac.  If it is connected to 100-200Vac power then it is only capable of 1500W output.  This is still plenty of power for most uses, but the limitations of the electrical infrastructure prevent a higher output wattage.

We were provided with a "Special Edition" model which is engraved(very nice FSP), but unfortunately "LTTLabs" won't be engraved on all production models.

This power supply has a hardware "ECO" switch on the rear of the chassis which allows you to enable/disable the Eco fan mode, allowing fanless operation under 40% load.  I don't think this is an advertised feature, but installing this power supply with a draw of less than 660W will theoretically turn it into a fanless power supply.

The power supply advertises all of the standard protections and we tested the SCP, OCP, and OPP.  Our first unit failed during the OCP testing of the 12V rail with 115V input.  We suspected, and confirmed with FSP Group, that this is because the power supply doesn't detect the input voltage and adjust the OCP trigger points to the lower wattage.(1500W)  Testing on the second unit skipped the 12V OCP test(with 115V input) and we collected OCP and OPP results for all other configurations.

With 230V input, the power supply was able to deliver 156A on the 12V rail alone(114% of full rating), and 122% of the rated load of the entire power supply when also loading the other output rails.  These values are lower than other power supplies we've tested but I suspect that this is due to the extremely high wattage and already pushing the limits of the input power.  Considering the high wattage and amperage, it is safer to have lower trigger points.

The minor rails of the power supply had quite low voltage ripple, but the 12V rail had quite a bit of variance compared to other 1600W+ power supplies we've tested.  This is unfortunate as this is the output rail that delivers most of the power.

The MEGA TI 1650W did show very good load regulation across the output rails, especially considering the large wattage range and high current that they're delivering.  

Excursion tests were conducted at 1500W on 115V and 1650W on 230V.  The performance was quite good with exception of the 100 ms excursions with 230V input.  These were likely triggering the protections and shutting down the power supply though I don't imagine people will be regularly running into these situations as it simulates quite a large simultaneous load.

name: Redmagic Gaming Mouse
text:
A surprisingly capable budget gaming mouse from a phone company that nails the basics and looks good doing it.

Inside the box, you get the mouse itself, a 2.4 GHz USB dongle, a braided USB-C to USB-A charging cable, an extra set of PTFE feet, and a quick start guide with pairing and setup instructions. The REDMAGIC mouse is made entirely of plastic with a glossy finish that gives it a flashy look but can feel slick during longer sessions, especially if your hands get sweaty. The build quality is solid overall. There is minimal flex or creaking, and the Kailh GM8.0 switches offer sharp, satisfying clicks with very little pretravel or posttravel. The scroll wheel is notched, but they have a subtle effect, and the side buttons are  easy to actuate. At around 75 g, it hits a middle ground that works for most grip styles without feeling too heavy or too light.

The REDMAGIC mouse offers button remapping, macro support, DPI adjustment, and RGB lighting controls through its Windows-only companion software called

REDMAGIC Cloud Smart Control

. No login is required, and settings are saved directly to the mouse. However, only one onboard profile is supported, and there's no cloud sync. It has a dongle storage which is always good to see. macOS users are out of luck since the software doesn’t support it. Despite those limits, the basics are well covered, especially for a mouse in this price range.

The REDMAGIC mouse uses the PixArt PAW3395 sensor with a DPI range from 50 to 26000, tracking speed of 650 IPS, and acceleration up to 50 g. In testing, it performed reliably across a range of sensitivities with no spin-outs or jitter. Latency measured at 4.72 ms when wired and 5.32 ms over 2.4 GHz wireless, which is competitive for its class. Bluetooth lagged behind at 11.02 ms, making it fine for casual use but not ideal for gaming. The polling rate caps out at 1000 Hz, which is standard but may be a step behind newer 4KHz mice.

Sound-wise, the Kailh GM8.0 switches produce a clean, sharp click with moderate volume. The scroll wheel has light tactile steps and a soft-sounding roll, while the side buttons have a slightly higher-pitched snap. Nothing sounds rattly or hollow, and the acoustics overall are inoffensive, leaning slightly on the loud side.

name: Razer Pro Click V2 Vertical Edition
text:
The Razer Pro Click V2 Vertical Edition is an ergonomic, vertical gaming mouse. It can be used in wired, 2.4 GHz wireless, or Bluetooth mode.

Razer only includes the bare essentials with the Pro Click V2 Vertical Edition. In the box you’ll find the mouse itself, a USB Type-A to Type-C cable, a Razer HyperSpeed Wireless dongle, and user documentation. Unlike many of Razer’s mice, the Pro Click V2 Vertical Edition’s cable is solid rather than braided. This is somewhat disappointing as braided cables can be easier to route from desk to computer, but shouldn’t be a major problem. The mouse features a rechargeable battery which Razer claims can last up to 6 months on a single charge.

Razer Synapse felt like one of the weak points of this mouse. The Pro Click V2 Vertical Edition only features a single on-board memory profile, but app-specific profiles can be configured within the software. These app profiles can be configured to automatically activate when an app is in use, or can be set to only apply manually.  In the mouse settings, the performance tab feels extremely barebones with nothing but sensitivity, polling rate, and mouse idle time adjustments available at the time of writing. There are a few more settings available in the lighting tab such as brightness, lighting effects, lighting idle time, and smart dimming. Unfortunately, if you really want to go crazy with custom lighting effects you’ll need the Razer Chroma software downloaded. Two programs to control a single mouse – you truly hate to see it.

The Razer Pro Click V2 Vertical Edition features the

Razer Focus Pro Optical Sensor

. The sensor is capable of resolutions from 100 to 30000 DPI with a minimum step of 50 DPI. Unfortunately, Labs was not able to verify the accuracy of the sensor due to the vertical orientation and height of the mouse. At the time of writing, vertical mice still do not fit in our DPI testing setup, but we are actively seeking solutions to this issue so stay tuned for future updates. 

The Pro Click V2 Vertical Edition boasts a 1000Hz polling rate which helps it deliver blazing fast response times when used wired or with the 2.4 GHz wireless receiver. In these modes we measured an average latency of 2.31 ms and 2.66 ms respectively. If you are a competitive gamer in search of an ergonomic solution, look no further, you’ll be hard pressed to find anything more responsive in this form factor.

name: Fnatic Bolt
text:
A sharp, lightweight wireless mouse that gets the essentials right but skips macro support.

The FNATIC Bolt ships with a 1.8 m braided USB-C to USB-A cable, a USB-A 2.4 GHz wireless receiver, a USB-C to USB-A adapter for dongle extension, and a spare set of PTFE feet. The accessory set covers most use cases out of the box, including both wired and wireless operation.

Build quality is strong for a mouse in this weight class. At the claimed mass of 67 g (We measured 69 g), it feels well put together with no noticeable flex, creaks, or internal rattle when handled. The matte plastic coating is smooth and comfortable to grip, although it may become slippery for users with sweaty hands. The Kailh GM 8.0 switches provide crisp clicks with minimal pre-travel and consistent tactility across both main buttons. The side buttons are decent but less defined than the primaries. The scroll wheel is quiet, has clear steps, and stays stable without wobble. The stock PTFE feet are rounded and glide smoothly, offering good control on both hard and soft mousepads.

The FNATIC Bolt uses

Fnatic OP

, a lightweight utility with no registration required. It supports four onboard profiles, button remapping, DPI adjustment from 50 to 19,000 in 50 DPI steps, lift-off distance control, polling rate changes, and macro programming. Debounce time is fixed, and there’s no cloud sync.

The interface is basic but functional, covering essential features without bloat. It works on both Windows and macOS. No RGB or flashy extras here, but the USB-C dongle extender is a nice touch. Battery life is rated at 110 hours on 2.4 GHz and 210 hours on Bluetooth, and charging is handled over USB-C.

Click latency comes in at 6.47 ms over 2.4 GHz wireless, which should be fast enough for most competitive scenarios. Wired mode improves slightly to 5.89 ms, while Bluetooth mode lags behind at 41.8 ms, making it more suitable for casual use. Polling rate is consistent at 1000 Hz with no major dips during testing.

Sensor accuracy is solid. At 800 DPI, the measured deviation is -2.03%, and the precision error is just 0.3%. Tracking remains stable even during fast swipes and quick flicks. DPI can be adjusted in 50 DPI increments from 100 to 19,000. Lift-off distance is under 1 mm by default and can be increased to 2 mm in the Fnatic OP software.

The sound profile is fairly balanced. The main clicks are crisp and quite loud, with a sharp upstroke and a solid return. The scroll wheel is quieter with firm, tactile steps. Side buttons sound a bit hollow but aren’t distracting in normal use.

name: XPG FUSION 1600 TITANIUM
text:
The XPG FUSION 1600 TITANIUM is a 1600W, fully modular, ATX 3.1 power supply developed in collaboration with Delta Electronics.

The power supply is packaged with plenty of accessories to complement the numerous cables.  In addition to the standard accessories it includes velcro cable ties, Molex to FDD adapters, a PRIME Port cable, and an OC Link Cable.  The OC Link cable can be used to link the PS_on signal of two units if 1600W isn't enough for you.  Naturally, the power supply has a very long case, and the fan location is biased toward the rear to focus on the power switching components.

The power supply has a 1600W rating, but this requires that the input voltage is in the range of 115-240V.  For the wider(and standard) range of 100-240V, it is only rated for 1300W output.  A split power rating is not uncommon for these high wattage power supplies.  The higher input voltage allows for lower current draw and less

waste heat

, enabling the higher output power.

The power supply has a single hybrid fan mode, carrying a 10 year warranty and eight advertised protections.  While two of these protections("Surge & Inrush Protection"(SIP) and "No Load Operation"(NLO)) are welcome, they are not tracked on this website as they are assumed to be implemented in a power supply.

The OCP and OPP results were very consistent.  The 12V, 5V, and 3.3V OCP, as well as the OPP triggered very consistently just below 130% of their rated loads.  This is slightly high, especially considering the high output rating of this power supply.  In the case of a fault it could allow quite a bit of power to be delivered before shutting down.

As a result of the 115-240V input voltage requirement, we did not conduct the 90V 47Hz tests.  The test results shown are considering the 1600W output rating.

We measured elevated voltage ripple/noise with the 115V input testing.  This is mostly seen in the minor/supplementary rails(5V, 3.3V, 5Vsb), but the ripple on the 12V rail is also slightly elevated for high loads.  These aren't alarming results but are certainly higher than the average.

The power supply performed quite well with voltage regulation and excursions.  While it failed to sustain a couple of the 100ms excursions(at 120% load), it was able to sustain those tests at different input voltages and temperatures.  It is likely that the power excursion demands of your equipment won't be an issue if you're staying within specifications.  

A contributing factor to the excursion and load regulation performance of the 12V rail is that the rail is biased above 12V.  We measured the 12V rail to go from roughly 12.25V at 0% load down to 12.1V at full load.  This higher voltage will allow the power supply to deliver slightly less current for the same wattage and also prevents the voltage from dipping too low during spikes(excursions) in load.

name: MCHOSE GX87
text:
A premium, hot-swappable, tri-mode TKL keyboard with a CNC aluminum body and shockingly good stock acoustics.

The GX87’s build quality is its standout trait. The CNC aluminum chassis gives it a premium, tank-like feel that most boards in this price range can’t touch. It’s weighty and feels like it could survive being used as a blunt weapon. Internally, the polycarbonate plate and multi-layer dampening work together to produce a refined sound profile without needing mods.

In the box, you get everything you actually need. The keyboard comes with a coiled USB-C to USB-A cable, a keycap and switch puller, the USB-A 2.4 GHz receiver, a set of extra switches, and documentation. Stock keycaps are decent but nothing exciting. They're PBT and feel fine to type on, but their OEM profile and generic look may have you reaching for replacements once you start customizing. The battery is massive at

8000 mAh

, and unless you're running full RGB all day, you’re unlikely to run it dry anytime soon.

One of the most overlooked highlights is the magnetic snap-fit disassembly system. No screws, no prying. Just press, and the case pops apart cleanly. The magnet is strong enough to prevent opening it up on accident. It’s weirdly fun and makes internal tweaks way less intimidating for beginners.

The GX87 supports VIA and QMK, so remapping and macros are simple and reliable without extra software bloat. VIA detects the board instantly and everything works as expected. Customization is deep if you want it to be, though there are no extras like encoders or screens. The RGB backlighting is functional but understated. South-facing LEDs play nice with the keycaps, and the lighting looks clean, especially on our silver and white model.

The MChose GX87 has a reasonable 1000Hz polling rate. It performs passably in our latency tests with a 10.8ms latency wired, 13.9ms latency over 2.4GHz receiver, and 25.0ms latency over bluetooth.

Our keystroke testing with the Vintage White linear switch showed a smooth curve with light actuation. The measured operating force was 48.15 gf, with a bottom-out at 52.26 gf. Pre-travel came in at 1.89 mm, with a total travel of 3.64 mm. The release force closely mirrors the press, giving it a stable and predictable return.

Sound-wise, the board has a genuinely deep and clean thock to it, aided by the case foam and polycarbonate plate. Our unit had no audible ping or hollowness, and several testers commented on how “modded” it sounded out of the box.

name: Pulsar TenZ Signature Edition/Red

specs.softwareName: Pulsar TenZ Signature Edition Wireless Mice Software

text:
The Pulsar TenZ SE is a hyper-light wireless gaming mouse co-designed by ex-pro CS:GO and Valorant player TenZ. Built for competitive claw grip users, it features an 8000 Hz polling rate, top-tier sensor, and no-nonsense design for serious aim performance.

In the box, you get everything you need and a little extra flair. Pulsar includes a USB Type-A to USB Type-C charging cable, an 8K wireless receiver, extra Pulsar dot skates and user documentation. The flair comes from a TenZ-themed sticker, and even a trading card for the fans.

Despite weighing just an alleged 47 g (we measured 48.9 g), the mouse feels solid. The matte finish is designed to resist sweat without turning slippery, and there’s zero flex or rattle anywhere.

The Pulsar TenZ SE uses the company’s

Signature Edition Wireless Mice Software

. There’s no required sign-in or registration, which is always appreciated. The software supports four onboard profiles. 

Additionally, the DPI, debounce time, liftoff distance, polling rate and the buttons are all customizable. Macro programming is available, but only on Windows. Everything saves to onboard memory, so once it’s set up, you don’t have to leave the software running or set it up separately on each device. That said, there’s no cloud sync or profile sharing.

The biggest standout is the 8000 Hz wireless polling rate, which solidifies its place among the serious gaming mice. Pulsar also mentions “Motion Sync” built into the XS‑1 sensor, which dynamically synchronizes the mouse’s sensor data transmission with the polling rate of the computer. It does this by ensuring that the mouse sensor data is only transferred when the computer requests it, eliminating potential delays and inconsistencies in data transfer.

In our testing, the mouse averaged 1.01 ms latency over the 2.4 GHz wireless receiver, putting it at the very front of the pack. The average wired latency came in at 2.12 ms, which is still solid, but it’s clear this mouse was tuned with wireless in mind. Bluetooth isn’t supported and honestly, we weren’t left wanting for it.

Tracking is handled by Pulsar’s XS‑1 sensor, rated for up to 32,000 DPI, 750 IPS, and 50 g acceleration. In our DPI testing, we found that the tracking is extremely consistent. DPI steps are adjustable in 10 DPI increments, and the lift-off distance sits comfortably at 0.7 mm by default, which should keep unwanted movement in check for low-sens players. The lift-off distance is adjustable across three levels: 0.7 mm, 1 mm, and 2 mm; not negative 2 mm, despite what

the website

with dramatic formatting might imply.

Click feel is sharp and fast thanks to Pulsar’s optical switches. They're not hot-swappable, but they performed consistently under stress testing. No wobble, no mush, just fast actuation and clean return. It was also specifically designed to be for the “spamming” play style so it definitely hits the mark there.

name: NZXT Lift 2 Symm
text:
A lightning-fast, featherweight, wired mouse that delivers elite performance for just $49.99 at the cost of sacrificing wirelessness.

This provides a no-frills unboxing experience. The Lift 2 Symm comes alone, without so much as a spare grip, sticker, or even a paper insert to keep it company. The documentation is available

online

. The construction is standard but sufficient, and while it doesn’t scream premium, it certainly is not bad enough to be a problem. At just 57g, it’s impressively light and easy to maneuver, ideal for competitive FPS players. The cable is rubber and non-detachable, which feels like a compromise, but a fair one given the wired-only design and the price tag. Overall, it’s a well-built, minimalist shell that puts performance over flair.

The Lift 2 Symm runs on

NZXT’s CAM software

, which doesn’t require registration and gives you control over polling rate, DPI settings, button remaps, and macro programming. It supports up to five onboard profiles, so your tweaks stick even when you switch machines. There’s no cloud sync, but most players won’t miss it. The only real limitation is that the software is Windows-only. This isn’t a huge loss, since most gamers aren’t fragging on a Mac anyway.

Performance is where the Lift 2 Symm throws punches well above its weight class. With an average wired latency of just 0.75 ms (the lowest we've tested) it's fast enough to register your click before you even second-guess it. That ridiculous speed is backed by an 8000 Hz polling rate and TTC optical switches, giving the mouse a sharp, satisfying click without debounce delay. The clicks are loud and crisp. While the switches aren't hot-swappable, they feel snappy and responsive under pressure.

The PixArt PMW3395 sensor holds its own, offering a 100 to 26,000 DPI range in 50 DPI increments. Tracking is smooth and consistent, and the lift-off distance is under 1 mm, so you shouldn’t see jitter when repositioning. For competitive players chasing every millisecond, this thing absolutely delivers. Just don’t expect silent clicks or forgiveness for bad aim.

name: Corsair RM1000e
text:
The Corsair RM1000e is a fully modular ATX 3.1 power supply which is advertised as Cybenetics Gold, but has actually achieved Cybenetics Platinum efficiency.

The RM1000e is an ATX form factor power supply with the standard depth of 140mm.  This keeps the footprint compact, but also limits the size of the fan.  In the Box, only standard accessories were included.  This likely helps to keep the cost of the power supply down and is a benefit to people who don't really need their power supply to come with velcro cable ties or a fancy cable bag.

The box and all of the insulation/packing is constructed of sturdy cardboard.  This is becoming a trend with Corsair's recent power supplies and it is very welcome.  It's easy to recycle, and we haven't experienced any issues with damage from insufficient support in shipping.

This power supply is advertised as a Cybenetics Gold, however, the Cybenetics test report shows that it has actually been evaluated as a Cybenetics Platinum at both 115V and 230V inputs.  This is likely because Corsair can't completely guarantee that each unit off the production line will meet this high performance, and they probably already printed the boxes with the gold label. (

this is funny, but not a joke

)

Unlike the RM1000x, this power supply only has a single fan mode.  This single fan mode is hybrid, which allows it to operate at low to medium loads without spinning the fan.  However, it removes the manual fan override knob from the rear of the chassis.  This is likely not much of a loss unless you feel the need to

turn your power supply fan to 11

while gaming.

All standard protections are present and well tuned. Over Power Protection (OPP) and Over Current Protection (OCP) on the 12V rail activate around 125%. The 5V and 3.3V rails are protected just below 150%.

Output voltage ripple/noise remain at reasonable levels and there should be no issues with stability.  The load regulation of the output rails is less impressive though, and the 12V rail has a variation of 0.308 V across the 1000W load range.  While it does stay within the ATX specification limits, this is quite a bit higher than other power supplies and is likely associated with the poor excursion performance.

The power supply failed to sustain two of the excursions (100ms duration).  Additionally, for the power excursions that it did sustain, the 12V rail dropped below the ATX minimum specification.  We measured the 12V rail to drop as low as 10.826V.

If greater stability and ripple reduction is required, the RM1000x provides better results in both of these tests and is likely a good choice.

name: Redragon Star Blade K707 Pro

specs.rgbSoftware: Star Blade K707 Pro Anime Keyboard Software
specs.softwareName: Star Blade K707 Pro Anime Keyboard Software

text:
The Redragon Star Blade K707 Pro, an 80% hot-swappable mechanical keyboard, doesn’t just flirt with anime flair, it charges in with enthusiast-tier specs and a waifu-approved otaku design that would feel right at home in a mecha cockpit.

In the box, the Star Blade Pro K707 includes a detachable USB Type-A to Type-C cable, a keycap and switch puller, a USB Type-A 2.4 GHz wireless receiver, four spare mint mambo switches, a physical user manual, and a Redragon sticker sheet. 

While the Star Blade K707 Pro boasts a gasket-mounted structure and multiple layers of foam, the overall construction doesn’t feel particularly premium. The plastic case feels lightweight and slightly hollow, and the finish lacks the heft or texture you'd find on higher-end boards. Despite the foam layering, we were divided about whether the keyboard sounds good. That said, for users coming from budget or membrane keyboards, the build quality might still feel like a solid step up. It’s functional and decently put together, even if it doesn’t hit enthusiast-grade refinement.

The Star Blade K707 Pro covers the basics, but it doesn't offer anything particularly standout in terms of features or software. It supports tri-mode connectivity (wired, Bluetooth, and 2.4 GHz wireless) with a toggle switch and onboard receiver/dongle storage, which is nice for flexibility. The keyboard has a standard companion app that handles the RGB customization like per key customization, and the macros.

LABS tested the Star Blade K707 Pro with Redragon’s factory-lubed Mint Mambo switches. These are linear switches designed to deliver a soft, cushioned keystroke with minimal resistance and a smooth downstroke. Redragon claims a deep sound profile, and the keyboard’s five layers of foam combined with its gasket-mounted design help to round out the acoustics. In practice, the typing experience leans more clacky than thocky, with a somewhat hollow undertone likely caused by the lightweight plastic case. We measured an operating force of 43.00 g, and 2.24 mm of pre-travel.

Featuring a 1000Hz polling rate, the Star Blade K707 Pro performed average in our latency testing. For the price this performance is to be expected, but the added latency might be noticeable for more hardcore gamers who are used to the ultra low latency from other brands such as Corsair or Razer.

name: MSI MAG A750GL PCIE5
text:
The MSI MAG A750GL PCIE5 is a fully modular ATX form factor power supply that is compliant with ATX 3.1.

In the box are the bare minimum accessories.  You do get a cable bag for the fairly standard complement of cables, handy for cables as well as the plethora of accessories and mounting hardware you collect with a new PC.  The cables have 'ribbon' construction and are moderately flexible, obviously more flexible in one direction than the other.

The stamped metal construction of the chassis is basic but sufficient for its purpose.  The ventilation in the top and rear should be sufficient for cooling, especially considering it has 80PLUS Gold efficiency and it only has a rating of 750W.

The power supply has a single fan mode where the fan is always spinning.  Missing here is any sort of hybrid fan mode so that it can operate without the fan for low loads.

The power supply is equipped with all of the protections and despite the first sample failing during the 3.3V OCP test, the second sample survived through all of the testing and had reasonable results.

The voltage ripple/noise on the 12V rail is slightly high for the lower loads but at higher loads it gets lower and is fairly average for the test results we've seen so far.  The ripple for the 5V and 3.3V rails is quite low.

The voltage regulation of the power supply is also very average.  I foresee no issues as a result of voltage instability during regular loads.  However, during excursion testing the CPU connector/cable on the 12V rail did drop down just below 11V for the 0.1 ms excursions.  This is below the ATX required minimum of 11.2V and may cause issues.  The 1.0 ms and 10 ms excursions also caused the 12V rail to dip below the ATX minimum.

name: Corsair MAKR 75
text:
The Corsair MAKR 75 is a fully hot swappable, 75%, mechanical keyboard which can be ordered as a barebones kit or as a fully assembled keyboard.

Given the choose-your-own-adventure nature of this keyboard, the components received in the box will depend almost entirely on your configuration. The barebones kit includes the keyboard (sans keycaps and switches), a USB Type-A to Type-C cable, User Documentation including a Warranty Guide and Safety and Compliance Information, a keycap and switch puller, a phillips screw driver, a hex key, additional screws, and extra gaskets.
